entry.title: Re: RCM - How to update postConvExtContactEmail
entry.content:
The postConvExtContactEmail field is intended to be read-only in the Candidate Profile XML.

It is the storage for the candidate’s external email after the conversion so it can be reused later during deconversion. In other words, this field is populated by the conversion process, not manually maintained.  

When an employee leaves the organization, the employee’s user account is inactivated. The corresponding internal candidate profile is converted to external candidate profile. A deconversion process does the conversion from internal to external candidate profile.

If an inactive employee has an internal candidate profile, a unique primary e-mail address is required to convert it to an external candidate profile. Using the primary e-mail address, the candidate can access the account in the external career site. To set a unique primary e-mail address for a candidate, the conversion job considers the following criteria for the deconversion:

  1. Restore the candidate’s primary e-mail address. (If external to internal conversion was done for the employee)

  2. Restore the candidate’s contact e-mail address, if the candidate's primary e-mail address doesn’t exist (external candidate profile wasn’t converted to internal candidate profile).

  3. Use employee's contact e-mail address if Candidate's contact e-mail address is unavailable or it exists for another user account.

    If any one of the conditions is met, the internal candidate profile is converted to an external candidate profile. If all the conditions fail, then an error occurs stating the duplicate profiles exist, or no contact e-mail address found for the employee.

    If duplicate candidate profile exists, use Manage Duplicate Candidates to merge them into one profile.

    After merging the duplicate profile or setting the unique e-mail address, if the conversion job doesn’t pick the inactivated candidate profile for deconversion, then set date of the conversion job.

    From Admin Center  Manage Recruiting Settings  Internal To External Candidate Profile conversion settings. Set the Employees marked as inactive in last [] weeks field value to set the last run date of the conversion job. Candidates inactivated on or after the set date are converted to external candidate when the conversion job is run.

Please refer to our guide: Internal to External Candidate Profile Deconversion

entry.title: Why Your SAP HANA System Replication Failover Works in Testing but Fails in Production
entry.content:
Why Your SAP HANA System Replication Failover Works in Testing but Fails in Production

Somewhere around my third or fourth HANA project, I started noticing something in the DR design docs: they almost all say some version of the same thing — System Replication gives you near-zero RPO and RTO under a minute. It's not a lie. I've run takeovers in sandbox and QA that finished in under sixty seconds more times than I can count. Green dashboard, clean logs, everyone nods, the document gets its sign-off.

Then the real thing happens. Not on a nice quiet Saturday morning either — usually it's 2am, half the bridge call is still finding their laptop, and the "sub-60-second" failover somehow eats forty minutes. Or HANA comes up primary just fine and nobody can actually log in. Or, worse, someone notices a batch of committed transactions that just aren't there anymore.

None of that is HANA's fault, by the way. In every case I've dug into afterward, the database did precisely what it was told to do. The mess was always somewhere else — a stale DNS cache, a connection pool that never learned to reconnect, an app server quietly queueing requests instead of failing over. I've hit this same pattern often enough across on-prem, RISE, and hyperscaler-hosted HANA that it seemed worth actually writing down, rather than re-explaining it from scratch on every new project.

Why bother writing about this

System Replication underpins nearly every HANA HA/DR strategy out there, whatever infrastructure it's sitting on. Cloud ALM assumes it's configured correctly. Your DR runbook assumes it's been tested. But "tested" in most shops means one clean, scheduled failover, run when nobody's actually using the system. That tells you almost nothing about the messy version — a primary that hangs instead of crashing outright, app servers with cached DNS entries that don't expire for several minutes, a secondary whose memory was never warmed up so takeover turns into a long cold start, or MDC tenants that drifted out of sync while SYSTEMDB looked perfectly fine the whole time.

Get any of that wrong once and it's not just an SLA miss. It's the next six months spent explaining to leadership why the expensive DR setup didn't behave the way the slide deck promised.

Quick thing worth knowing: systemReplicationStatus.py only reports what the primary believes is happening. If the primary itself has gone dark, which is sort of the whole point of building HA in the first place, that script tells you nothing at all. Your failover logic has to rely on something that doesn't depend on the primary being reachable — an external HA/DR provider hook, not a person SSHing in to run a query after the fact.

The setup, roughly

Most enterprise landscapes I work with run a fairly standard three-tier layout — sync to a local secondary, async out to a DR site somewhere else entirely:

                 ┌───────────────────────────┐
                 │        PRIMARY (Site A)    │
                 │  HANA DB : Active/Primary  │
                 │  Mode: SYNC / SYNCMEM       │
                 └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                                │  Log Shipping (sync)
                                ▼
                 ┌───────────────────────────┐
                 │      SECONDARY (Site B)    │
                 │  HANA DB : Hot Standby     │
                 │  Mode: SYNC / SYNCMEM       │
                 │  Operation Mode: logreplay  │
                 └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                                │  Log Shipping (async)
                                ▼
                 ┌───────────────────────────┐
                 │       TERTIARY (Site C)    │
                 │  HANA DB : DR Standby      │
                 │  Mode: ASYNC                │
                 │  Operation Mode: logreplay  │
                 └───────────────────────────┘

That picture is fine, as far as it goes. But it's also usually where the architecture conversation ends, and that's the actual problem. What decides your real RTO isn't really pictured above at all — it's the replication mode you picked, whether the operation mode is logreplay or something lazier, whether Pacemaker or your cloud's native HA tooling is actually watching things closely enough, whether there's a virtual IP or DNS entry that will converge fast, whether your app servers are configured to fail fast and retry instead of queue, and whether anyone's actually watching the business-visible side of things through something like Cloud ALM.

I've started telling people: if it's not on the diagram, it doesn't get tested. So draw it all the way up to the logon group and whatever's doing health checks on your load balancer. Every failed production failover I've had to pick apart afterward turned out to be a network or application problem, never a HANA problem.

The one that stuck with me

There was a financial services customer — I won't get into which one — running what looked like a textbook setup. Sync secondary in the same availability zone, async DR site about 400km away, quarterly failover tests that always came in under 90 seconds. Everyone, understandably, felt good about it.

Then a storage controller firmware bug caused the primary host to hang. Not crash — hang. It kept answering pings, but any actual SQL call just sat there timing out. Pacemaker's resource agent caught it and triggered a takeover to the sync secondary, which was open and accepting connections in 45 seconds. Genuinely a good number.

Except the core switch's ARP cache took almost four minutes to let go of the old virtual IP, because the dying host was still intermittently answering at Layer 2. The ABAP application servers had rdisp/wait_for_rfc set at 300 seconds, so instead of failing and retrying against the new primary, they just sat there queueing work. And a custom Java interface on the SAP JDBC driver had a connection pool with no reconnect logic at all — it kept retrying the dead IP for a solid ten minutes before someone on the call finally just bounced the service by hand.

Total business-visible downtime ended up around 27 minutes against a contractual RTO of five. HANA did its part in under a minute. The other 26 minutes belonged entirely to pieces nobody had thought to include in the test plan. That's the project that convinced me to push, on every engagement since, for at least one unannounced failover test with real connection load, not just the polite Saturday version everyone's expecting.

Getting it right in practice

Here's roughly the sequence I walk through now, which goes further than the standard SAP guide precisely because it covers the parts that keep causing the actual damage.

Start by baselining where replication actually stands:

# Run as <sid>adm on the primary
python systemReplicationStatus.py

# For MDC systems, check tenant-level registration explicitly
hdbsql -i 00 -u SYSTEM -d SYSTEMDB "SELECT DATABASE_NAME, SECONDARY_HOST, REPLICATION_STATUS, REPLICATION_MODE FROM SYS.M_DATABASE_REPLICATION"

On MDC systems, always check tenant-level status separately. I've seen SYSTEMDB report ACTIVE while a tenant quietly fell out of sync after an unrelated restart, and nobody noticed for weeks.

Next, check whether the operation mode actually matches what you're promising for RTO:

SELECT SITE_NAME, SECONDARY_ACTIVE_STATUS, REPLICATION_MODE, OPERATION_MODE
FROM SYS.M_SERVICE_REPLICATION;

If this comes back delta_datashipping instead of logreplay, the secondary isn't continuously preloading data, so it'll cold-load at takeover time. On a small QA box that barely matters. On a large production system it's often the single biggest reason a failover that took forty seconds in the lab takes twenty-plus minutes for real.

Then the cluster layer:

crm_mon -1 -Af
cibadmin -Q | grep -A 20 "rsc_SAPHana"
grep -A 3 "\[ha_dr_provider_SAPHanaSR\]" /hana/shared/<SID>/global/hdb/custom/config/global.ini

Confirm the hook is actually registered and reporting — hdbnsutil -sr_state should agree with what Pacemaker thinks. A misconfigured hook is a nasty failure mode because everything looks fine right up until it isn't.

The step almost everyone skips is testing network convergence on its own, separately from the database entirely:

watch -n 1 "arp -n | grep <virtual_ip>"

# Cloud example (Azure route table)
az network route-table route show --resource-group <rg> --route-table-name <rt> --name <route-name>

Time how long it takes for the network, specifically, to converge on the new primary. On more than one project this number was bigger than the database takeover — which tells you something about where the DR budget probably should have gone in the first place.

Then check whether the application layer is actually going to reconnect rather than just sit there waiting:

grep -E "rdisp/wait_for_rfc|rsdb/reco" $DIR_PROFILE/DEFAULT.PFL

Worth checking SMLG too, to confirm logon groups actually route users somewhere useful once things move. Application servers using HANA's DBSL will reconnect on their own once DNS/vIP converges, but only if dbs/hdb/timeout_reconnect is tuned for what you're actually promising — the out-of-the-box setting tends to be more conservative than most RTO targets can afford.

And last, put real monitoring on top of all of it through Cloud ALM — a lightweight synthetic transaction, something that tells you when the business can actually work again, not just when the database says it's open. That's the number worth reporting up the chain, not the green checkmark from a script.

Commands and config in one place, since I always end up looking these up anyway

Purpose Command / transaction

Replication status (primary side)python systemReplicationStatus.py
Per-tenant replication statusSELECT * FROM SYS.M_DATABASE_REPLICATION
Service-level replication modeSELECT * FROM SYS.M_SERVICE_REPLICATION
Manual takeoverhdbnsutil -sr_takeover
Local SR statehdbnsutil -sr_state
Re-register old primary as secondaryhdbnsutil -sr_register --remoteHost=<host> --remoteInstance=<nr> --replicationMode=sync --name=<siteName>
Cluster statuscrm_mon -1 -Af
Resource locationcrm_resource --resource rsc_SAPHana_<SID>_HDB<NR> --locate
Logon group configSMLG
Profile parametersRZ11
Replication dashboardHANA Cockpit → System Replication
Synthetic business monitoringCloud ALM → Business Process Monitoring
[ha_dr_provider_SAPHanaSR]
provider = SAPHanaSR
path = /usr/share/SAPHanaSR/srHook
execution_order = 1

[trace]
ha_dr_saphanasr = info

The mistakes I keep seeing, project after project

The most common one, by far, is leaving the operation mode on delta_datashipping and not realizing it until a large production failover runs long. Second is only checking SYSTEMDB and assuming the tenants are fine — they're often not, and nothing will tell you unless you specifically go looking. DNS TTLs left too high are another classic; app servers keep hammering an address that's already dead. Default reconnect timeouts are almost always too generous for anyone with a serious RTO. And a lot of teams simply never run an unannounced test — everything's scheduled, everything's calm, and then reality doesn't cooperate.

I'd also add: check the tertiary site. Multi-target replication chains have a way of quietly breaking somewhere in the middle and nobody notices for months, because everyone's watching primary-to-secondary and the DR-of-the-DR gets forgotten.

When a takeover technically "succeeds" but people still can't work, here's roughly the order I check things:

  • New primary actually accepting connections (hdbsql ... SELECT * FROM DUMMY)
  • Virtual IP or DNS genuinely converged, from the app server's point of view, not the database's
  • Work processes reconnecting instead of queueing — worth a look at the dev_w* traces
  • Logon groups routing correctly through SMLG
  • Any custom middleware connection pools actually reconnected, not just retrying forever
  • Cloud ALM synthetic transaction back to green

A few performance things worth knowing

Log buffer sizing gets overlooked more than it should. Undersized buffers mean more frequent segment switches, which can momentarily stall sync replication under real load — size for peak throughput, not the average day. For an async tertiary, keep an eye on available bandwidth; if it's not enough, the log backlog just quietly grows and your real RPO ends up worse than the configuration implies, and you won't know until you check SHIPPED_LOG_BUFFERS_COUNT and the backlog size directly. After a takeover, M_CS_TABLES will show you what's actually loaded — a system can report itself open while the tables people actually need are still cold. And don't schedule a delta merge to land during your DR test window; it'll throw your numbers off in one direction or another and you'll never quite know which.

Small thing that catches people out: logreplay_readaccess lets you run reporting against the secondary, which sounds great for getting some use out of otherwise idle hardware. But it also means more stays warm in memory over there, so the secondary needs sizing like it's doing real work — not a passive standby. I've seen this missed during the original sizing exercise more than once, and it's an awkward conversation to have after the fact.

And a bit on security

Turn on enable_ssl for replication traffic, especially anything crossing to a DR site outside the trusted network, and actually own certificate renewal somewhere central — an expired cert breaks replication with an error message that gives you almost no hint what actually went wrong. Don't assume the secondary inherits the same authorizations and audit configuration as the primary; verify it, particularly anything hardcoded like hdbuserstore entries pointing at a physical hostname rather than the logical one. And check audit logging continuity after a takeover — a failover shouldn't quietly create a gap in your compliance trail just because the secondary's config drifted from the primary's.

What I'd actually tell a team building this today

Default to logreplay unless there's a specific cost reason not to. Let cluster software drive the takeover — manual hdbnsutil -sr_takeover should be the backup plan, never the plan. Put the network and application layers on the same architecture diagram as the database and hold them to the same testing standard. Run at least one unannounced failover a year under real load, not just the scheduled kind. Report recovery time the way the business experiences it, through Cloud ALM, rather than the database-only number. And take reintegration — putting the old primary back as a secondary via hdbnsutil -sr_register — just as seriously as the takeover itself, because that's where I've watched teams get burned a second time.

Before go-live, I'd want to see the operation mode matched to the actual RTO target, tenant-level replication validated on any MDC system, the cluster tested against a genuine hard failure and not just a graceful stop, network convergence measured on its own, app server reconnect settings actually tuned and tested, Cloud ALM monitoring live, SSL enabled with someone clearly owning renewal, an unannounced test on the calendar, and failback documented and rehearsed — not just written down somewhere nobody's opened since go-live.

What I've taken away from doing this a few times now

A green replication status is necessary, but it's nowhere near sufficient — I treat it as one data point among several these days, not the whole story. Application teams are often nowhere near the room when HA/DR gets planned, and yet their connection pool settings are frequently what actually determines the real recovery time; getting them involved early is worth the awkward extra meeting. Planned drills build a kind of false confidence — every genuinely surprising production failover I've been pulled into had passed several scheduled tests beforehand, which tells you the tests weren't measuring the right thing. Sizing the secondary like a full production peer, not a scaled-down afterthought, pays for itself the first time logreplay actually has to keep pace with real throughput. And reintegration is where the second outage tends to happen — more than one customer I've supported failed over cleanly, then broke replication again during failback because nobody rehearsed that half with the same care as the first half.

The short version, if you're skimming

HANA System Replication itself is solid. Most of the pain in a real failover comes from what's built around it, not the database engine. Use logreplay when RTO actually matters. Test under real, unannounced conditions at least once a year — a quiet Saturday morning tells you very little about a 2am incident. Measure recovery the way the business experiences it, not the way the database reports it. And give reintegration the same respect as the takeover.

References

  • SAP HANA Administration Guide — System Replication chapter, SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Note 1999880 – FAQ: SAP HANA System Replication
  • SAP Note 2407186 – How-To Guides & Whitepapers for SAP HANA High Availability
  • SAP HANA SR cluster guides for SUSE/Red Hat, via SAP's HA/DR partner documentation
  • Cloud ALM Help Portal — Business Process Monitoring

A few questions people usually ask me

Does logreplay mean there's no cold-load impact at all? Not quite. It keeps replicated tables continuously loaded on the secondary, which helps enormously, but anything not yet loaded on the primary at the moment of replication won't be preloaded on the secondary either. Full parity still depends on what the primary itself has loaded.

Should the local secondary always be SYNC? For most Tier-1 systems, yes, if zero RPO is genuinely the requirement. Just know it makes write latency sensitive to network conditions between the sites, so test it under real peak load rather than an idle system where everything looks fast.

Can Cloud ALM trigger the failover itself? No — it's monitoring and alerting, including synthetic business checks. The actual failover mechanics should stay with cluster software or your cloud provider's native HA service.

How often is often enough for testing? Quarterly planned tests at a minimum, plus at least one unannounced test a year under something resembling real load. If there's a tertiary DR site, test it on the same schedule — it's easy to let that one slide for months without anyone noticing.

Curious whether others have run into this same pattern — a takeover that was clean at the database level but still cost real downtime because of something in the network or application layer. What's in your unannounced-test runbook, and how do you get the application teams into the room early enough for it to matter? Would genuinely like to hear what's out there.

entry.title: Subject: Can anyone help with recording Inspection Lot defects from an SE38 custom program
entry.content:
Subject: Can anyone help with recording Inspection Lot defects from an SE38 custom program ?

Hi Experts,

I am working on a custom ABAP report (SE38) for SAP QM.

My requirement is to perform a partial reversal of Usage Decision. The stock reversal part is already working successfully.

After the partial reversal, I need to record a manual defect against the Inspection Lot from the same custom report.

I investigated the standard SAP QM interface and found the function module QIRF_GET_DEFECT_ITEMS2. According to SAP QM-IDI documentation, this FM is used to confirm defect items using record types such as Q90 (Inspection Lot), Q91 (Operation), Q92 (Characteristic), Q95, and Q96.

I have also debugged the standard implementation and found that:

  • QIRF_GET_DEFECT_ITEMS2

  • → DEFECT_ITEM_RECORDING

  • → QFFE_DEFECT_ITEMS_GENER_LOT

I am populating the QMIFE structure and calling the FM from my SE38 report.

However, I currently cannot complete end-to-end testing because there is no QM inspection lot test data available in my DEV system.

My questions are:

  1. Is QIRF_GET_DEFECT_ITEMS2 the correct SAP-standard FM to record manual defects from a custom SE38 program?

  2. Has anyone successfully used this FM from a custom report (not from QE51N or another standard transaction)?

  3. For Inspection Lot defects (Q90), which QMIFE fields are mandatory?

  4. Is there any SAP-recommended alternative API/BAPI for this requirement?

Any sample code or working example would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you

entry.title: Why Your SAP HANA System Replication Failover Works in Testing but Fails in Production
entry.content:
Why Your SAP HANA System Replication Failover Works in Testing but Fails in Production

Somewhere around my third or fourth HANA project, I started noticing something in the DR design docs: they almost all say some version of the same thing — System Replication gives you near-zero RPO and RTO under a minute. Then check whether the application layer is actually going to reconnect rather than just sit there waiting:

grep -E "rdisp/wait_for_rfc|rsdb/reco" $DIR_PROFILE/DEFAULT.PFL

Worth checking SMLG too, to confirm logon groups actually route users somewhere useful once things move. Application servers using HANA's DBSL will reconnect on their own once DNS/vIP converges, but only if dbs/hdb/timeout_reconnect is tuned for what you're actually promising — the out-of-the-box setting tends to be more conservative than most RTO targets can afford.

And last, put real monitoring on top of all of it through Cloud ALM — a lightweight synthetic transaction, something that tells you when the business can actually work again, not just when the database says it's open. That's the number worth reporting up the chain, not the green checkmark from a script.

Commands and config in one place, since I always end up looking these up anyway

Purpose Command / transaction

Replication status (primary side)python systemReplicationStatus.py
Per-tenant replication statusSELECT * FROM SYS.M_DATABASE_REPLICATION
Service-level replication modeSELECT * FROM SYS.M_SERVICE_REPLICATION
Manual takeoverhdbnsutil -sr_takeover
Local SR statehdbnsutil -sr_state
Re-register old primary as secondaryhdbnsutil -sr_register --remoteHost=<host> --remoteInstance=<nr> --replicationMode=sync --name=<siteName>
Cluster statuscrm_mon -1 -Af
Resource locationcrm_resource --resource rsc_SAPHana_<SID>_HDB<NR> --locate
Logon group configSMLG
Profile parametersRZ11
Replication dashboardHANA Cockpit → System Replication
Synthetic business monitoringCloud ALM → Business Process Monitoring
[ha_dr_provider_SAPHanaSR]
provider = SAPHanaSR
path = /usr/share/SAPHanaSR/srHook
execution_order = 1

[trace]
ha_dr_saphanasr = info

The mistakes I keep seeing, project after project

The most common one, by far, is leaving the operation mode on delta_datashipping and not realizing it until a large production failover runs long. Second is only checking SYSTEMDB and assuming the tenants are fine — they're often not, and nothing will tell you unless you specifically go looking. DNS TTLs left too high are another classic; app servers keep hammering an address that's already dead. Default reconnect timeouts are almost always too generous for anyone with a serious RTO. And a lot of teams simply never run an unannounced test — everything's scheduled, everything's calm, and then reality doesn't cooperate.

I'd also add: check the tertiary site. Multi-target replication chains have a way of quietly breaking somewhere in the middle and nobody notices for months, because everyone's watching primary-to-secondary and the DR-of-the-DR gets forgotten.

When a takeover technically "succeeds" but people still can't work, here's roughly the order I check things:

  • New primary actually accepting connections (hdbsql ... SELECT * FROM DUMMY)
  • Virtual IP or DNS genuinely converged, from the app server's point of view, not the database's
  • Work processes reconnecting instead of queueing — worth a look at the dev_w* traces
  • Logon groups routing correctly through SMLG
  • Any custom middleware connection pools actually reconnected, not just retrying forever
  • Cloud ALM synthetic transaction back to green

A few performance things worth knowing

Log buffer sizing gets overlooked more than it should. Undersized buffers mean more frequent segment switches, which can momentarily stall sync replication under real load — size for peak throughput, not the average day. For an async tertiary, keep an eye on available bandwidth; if it's not enough, the log backlog just quietly grows and your real RPO ends up worse than the configuration implies, and you won't know until you check SHIPPED_LOG_BUFFERS_COUNT and the backlog size directly. After a takeover, M_CS_TABLES will show you what's actually loaded — a system can report itself open while the tables people actually need are still cold. And don't schedule a delta merge to land during your DR test window; it'll throw your numbers off in one direction or another and you'll never quite know which.

Small thing that catches people out: logreplay_readaccess lets you run reporting against the secondary, which sounds great for getting some use out of otherwise idle hardware. But it also means more stays warm in memory over there, so the secondary needs sizing like it's doing real work — not a passive standby. I've seen this missed during the original sizing exercise more than once, and it's an awkward conversation to have after the fact.

And a bit on security

Turn on enable_ssl for replication traffic, especially anything crossing to a DR site outside the trusted network, and actually own certificate renewal somewhere central — an expired cert breaks replication with an error message that gives you almost no hint what actually went wrong. Don't assume the secondary inherits the same authorizations and audit configuration as the primary; verify it, particularly anything hardcoded like hdbuserstore entries pointing at a physical hostname rather than the logical one. And check audit logging continuity after a takeover — a failover shouldn't quietly create a gap in your compliance trail just because the secondary's config drifted from the primary's.

What I'd actually tell a team building this today

Default to logreplay unless there's a specific cost reason not to. Let cluster software drive the takeover — manual hdbnsutil -sr_takeover should be the backup plan, never the plan. Put the network and application layers on the same architecture diagram as the database and hold them to the same testing standard. Run at least one unannounced failover a year under real load, not just the scheduled kind. Report recovery time the way the business experiences it, through Cloud ALM, rather than the database-only number. And take reintegration — putting the old primary back as a secondary via hdbnsutil -sr_register — just as seriously as the takeover itself, because that's where I've watched teams get burned a second time.

Before go-live, I'd want to see the operation mode matched to the actual RTO target, tenant-level replication validated on any MDC system, the cluster tested against a genuine hard failure and not just a graceful stop, network convergence measured on its own, app server reconnect settings actually tuned and tested, Cloud ALM monitoring live, SSL enabled with someone clearly owning renewal, an unannounced test on the calendar, and failback documented and rehearsed — not just written down somewhere nobody's opened since go-live.

What I've taken away from doing this a few times now

A green replication status is necessary, but it's nowhere near sufficient — I treat it as one data point among several these days, not the whole story. Application teams are often nowhere near the room when HA/DR gets planned, and yet their connection pool settings are frequently what actually determines the real recovery time; getting them involved early is worth the awkward extra meeting. Planned drills build a kind of false confidence — every genuinely surprising production failover I've been pulled into had passed several scheduled tests beforehand, which tells you the tests weren't measuring the right thing. Sizing the secondary like a full production peer, not a scaled-down afterthought, pays for itself the first time logreplay actually has to keep pace with real throughput. And reintegration is where the second outage tends to happen — more than one customer I've supported failed over cleanly, then broke replication again during failback because nobody rehearsed that half with the same care as the first half.

The short version, if you're skimming

HANA System Replication itself is solid. Most of the pain in a real failover comes from what's built around it, not the database engine. Use logreplay when RTO actually matters. Test under real, unannounced conditions at least once a year — a quiet Saturday morning tells you very little about a 2am incident. Measure recovery the way the business experiences it, not the way the database reports it. And give reintegration the same respect as the takeover.

References

  • SAP HANA Administration Guide — System Replication chapter, SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Note 1999880 – FAQ: SAP HANA System Replication
  • SAP Note 2407186 – How-To Guides & Whitepapers for SAP HANA High Availability
  • SAP HANA SR cluster guides for SUSE/Red Hat, via SAP's HA/DR partner documentation
  • Cloud ALM Help Portal — Business Process Monitoring

A few questions people usually ask me

Does logreplay mean there's no cold-load impact at all? Not quite. It keeps replicated tables continuously loaded on the secondary, which helps enormously, but anything not yet loaded on the primary at the moment of replication won't be preloaded on the secondary either. Full parity still depends on what the primary itself has loaded.

Should the local secondary always be SYNC? For most Tier-1 systems, yes, if zero RPO is genuinely the requirement. Just know it makes write latency sensitive to network conditions between the sites, so test it under real peak load rather than an idle system where everything looks fast.

Can Cloud ALM trigger the failover itself? No — it's monitoring and alerting, including synthetic business checks. The actual failover mechanics should stay with cluster software or your cloud provider's native HA service.

How often is often enough for testing? Quarterly planned tests at a minimum, plus at least one unannounced test a year under something resembling real load. If there's a tertiary DR site, test it on the same schedule — it's easy to let that one slide for months without anyone noticing.

Curious whether others have run into this same pattern — a takeover that was clean at the database level but still cost real downtime because of something in the network or application layer. What's in your unannounced-test runbook, and how do you get the application teams into the room early enough for it to matter? Would genuinely like to hear what's out there.

entry.title: Clarification on Using BW DataSources in Datasphere Migration
entry.content:
Hello SAP Team,

We are currently migrating our BW data flows to SAP Datasphere.

During this migration, we have encountered a challenge in identifying appropriate CDS views for certain objects. Some of the required fields are custom fields that were previously used in BW DSOs, and for these cases we are currently relying on BW DataSources (For Example: 0HR_PA_0, 0HR_PA_1, 0HRPOSITION_ATTR and 0EC_PCA_1). 

Can we use these Datasources or do we need to create CDS views ? Which one is preferable ?

However, we are unable to identify equivalent CDS views or the corresponding underlying tables for some of these fields.

Could you please confirm whether using BW DataSources in such scenarios is considered a recommended or acceptable best practice when equivalent CDS views are not available?

Additionally, if it is acceptable to use BW DataSources in these cases, could you also confirm whether the same approach can be applied consistently across other functional modules where similar limitations exist?

We would appreciate your guidance on this matter.

Thank you in advance for your support.

 

Regards,
Radhakrushna

entry.title: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 2 — Roll & Move
entry.content:
Week 2 — Roll & Move

Challenge series: Snake & Ladder Developer Challenge | Week 2 of 4 Dates: 13–19 July 2026

The Setup

You have a board. You have a player state machine. This week we will make it move.

This week you implement the bound action handlers that power the game: dice rolling, position calculation, and snake/ladder logic. CAP's @flow.status machinery will automatically enforce that moves only happen in the right state — your job is to write the business logic inside each handler.

By the end of this week you can play a full single-player game from the command line using plain OData calls.

What You'll Build This Week

  • Handler implementations for rollDice, confirmMove, blockPlayer, unblockPlayer, and winGame
  • Snake and ladder position resolution (including double-headed snake logic)
  • HTTP 409 proof: demonstrating that calling an action in the wrong state is rejected by CAP
  • An end-to-end single-player run reaching square 100

How CAP Enforces State Transitions

Before your handler code runs, CAP checks the current TurnStatus of the player entity against the @from annotation on the action. If the status doesn't match, CAP returns:

HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
{
  "error": {
    "code": "409",
    "message": "Action 'rollDice' is not allowed in current status 'Waiting'"
  }
}

No code from you. No if (status !== 'Playing') throw .... The annotation does it.

After your handler returns (without throwing), CAP updates the status field to the @TO value. Again — no code from you.

This is the key insight of the week: your handler only contains business logic, not state management.

Step-by-Step

1. Create the service implementation file

Create srv/game-service.js:

const cds = require('@sap/cds')

module.exports = class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  async init() {

    const { Players, BoardSquares } = this.entities

    // ── startTurn ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Waiting]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @to: #Playing      →  CAP updates status after this returns
    // No business logic needed — CAP's @flow.status handles the transition.
    this.on('startTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Intentionally empty: the @from/@to annotations do all the work.
    })

    // ── rollDice ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Playing]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @to: #Moving       →  CAP updates status after this returns
    this.on('rollDice', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      const roll = Math.ceil(Math.random() * 6)
      let newPosition = player.position + roll

      // Can't overshoot square 100 — must land exactly
      if (newPosition > 100) {
        await UPDATE(Players).set({ lastRoll: roll }).where({ ID: player.ID })
        const needed = 100 - player.position
        return req.reject(409, `Need exactly ${needed} to finish. Rolled ${roll}. No move.`)
      }

      // Resolve snake or ladder on the new square
      const square = await SELECT.one.from(BoardSquares).where({ square: newPosition })
      let finalPosition = newPosition
      let event = null

      if (square?.ladderTo) {
        finalPosition = square.ladderTo
        event = { type: 'ladder', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      } else if (square?.snakeTo) {
        finalPosition = square.snakeTo
        event = square.isDoubleHead
          ? { type: 'doubleSnake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
          : { type: 'snake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      }

      // Persist position and last roll
      await UPDATE(Players)
        .set({ position: finalPosition, lastRoll: roll })
        .where({ ID: player.ID })

      // Emit domain event so subscribers (Week 3) can react
      if (event) await this.emit('BoardEvent', { playerID: player.ID, ...event })

      // Use req.reply() — not return — to pin the response value before CAP's
      // @flow.status after-handler runs. A plain `return` gets cleared by the
      // flow machinery, producing HTTP 204 instead of the expected 200 + body.
      req.reply({ roll, position: finalPosition, event: event?.type ?? 'normal' })
    })

    // ── confirmMove ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  normal end of a turn, hands off to next player (Week 3)
    // @to: #Waiting     →  player waits for their next turn
    this.on('confirmMove', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Week 3 will add turn rotation here.
      // For now: just let CAP apply @to: #Waiting
    })

    // ── blockPlayer ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player lands on a double-headed snake
    // @to: #Blocked     →  player skips their next turn
    this.on('blockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Business logic: the position was already updated in rollDice.
      // This action just transitions state — CAP handles the @to: #Blocked update.
      req.info('Player is blocked and will skip their next turn.')
    })

    // ── unblockPlayer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
    // @to: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
    this.on('unblockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // CAP's $flow.previous does the work — no code needed here.
      // The player's status returns to #Waiting automatically.
    })

    // ── winGame ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player.position === 100
    // @to: #Finished    →  player has won
    this.on('winGame', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      // Mark the session as finished with this player as winner
      await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
        .set({
          finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
          winner_ID: player.ID
        })
        .where({ ID: player.session_ID })

      await this.emit('GameWon', { playerID: player.ID, sessionID: player.session_ID })
    })

    return super.init()
  }
}

If you notice rollDice action response looks different from how it was defined last week.

Lets adapt it as shown in srv/game-service.cds

  @from: [ #Playing ]
  @to: #Moving
  action rollDice() returns { roll: Integer; position: Integer; event: String; };

 

2. Add the domain events to your service CDS

Add these to the bottom of srv/game-service.cds:

// Domain events — emitted by handlers, consumed in Week 3
event BoardEvent {
  playerID : UUID;
  type     : String;   // 'ladder' | 'snake' | 'doubleSnake'
  from  : Integer;  // 'from' is a CDS reserved word — escape with ![]
  ![to]    : Integer;
}

event GameWon {
  playerID  : UUID;
  sessionID : UUID;
}

An error is intentionally introduced in the snippet above with the hint.  

3. Test the state machine manually

Start the server:

cds watch

First, create a player using the REST API or the Fiori Launchpad at http://localhost:4004. Then test the status flow:

Note on service path: CAP derives the OData path from the service name. GameService maps to /odata/v4/game (lowercase, stripped of the "Service" suffix). All example requests below use this path. You can confirm the mounted path in the cds watch console output: serving GameService { at: '/odata/v4/game' }.

Step 1 — Start a turn (transitions Waiting → Playing):

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)/startTurn
Content-Type: application/json
{}

Expected response: HTTP 204 No Content (empty body). This is correct — startTurn has no return type, so CAP applies the @TO: #Playing transition silently. Verify the state changed with a GET:

GET http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/GameService/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)?$select=TurnStatus

You should see "TurnStatus": "Playing".

Step 2 — Roll the dice (transitions Playing → Moving):

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)/rollDice
Content-Type: application/json
{}

Expected response: HTTP 200 with the dice roll, new position, and board event:

{
  "@context": "$metadata#GameService.rollDiceType",
  "roll": 4,
  "position": 38,
  "event": "ladder"
}

The event field is "normal", "ladder", "snake", or "doubleSnake". The position is the final square after any snake or ladder has been applied — so you can read the outcome directly from the action response without a follow-up GET.

Why req.reply() not return? CAP's @flow.status machinery runs an after-handler that updates the status field. If you use a plain return, that after-handler clears the response value and the client gets HTTP 204 No Content instead. Calling req.reply({...}) pins the response before the flow handler runs, so the body reaches the client.

Step 3 — Prove the 409 rejection:

Without calling confirmMove first, try to call rollDice again. CAP will return:

HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
{
  "error": {
    "code": "409",
    "message": "Action 'rollDice' is not allowed in current status 'Moving'"
  }
}

This is the framework working exactly as designed. Screenshot this — it's your Week 2 submission evidence.

Step 4 — Confirm the move (transitions Moving → Waiting):

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)/confirmMove
Content-Type: application/json
{}

Expected response: HTTP 204 No Content (empty body). confirmMove has no return type — the empty response is correct. Verify with a GET that TurnStatus is now "Waiting".

4. Test the double-headed snake

Manually set a player to position 88 (one roll away from square 89, a double-headed snake head):

PATCH http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/GameService/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)
Content-Type: application/json
{ "position": 88 }

Note: position is @readonly in the service — for testing, update it directly via the db entity or temporarily remove @readonly.

Then start a turn and roll — if you roll 1, you land on 89 (a double-headed snake head). Your position should move to 50 (the shared tail). The BoardEvent event should fire with type: 'doubleSnake'.

Follow up by calling blockPlayer to transition to #Blocked, then unblockPlayer to see $flow.previous restore the state.

The $flow.previous Moment

When you call unblockPlayer and see the status return to its previous value automatically — that's CAP's state history at work. No code, no stored "previousStatus" field. The framework tracks it.

Note: $flow.previous restores whatever status the entity held before the transition to #Blocked. In this flow, blockPlayer is called from #Moving, so unblockPlayer restores #Moving — not #Waiting. The game loop then calls confirmMove to finish the turn. If you call blockPlayer from a different status in future extensions, $flow.previous will restore that one instead.

Stretch Goal

Add a skipTurn action that can be called when a player is #Blocked, as an alternative to unblockPlayer, that uses @TO: $flow.previous as well but also increments a turnsBlocked counter on the player:

@from: [ #Blocked ]
@to: $flow.previous
action skipTurn();
this.on('skipTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
  const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
  await UPDATE(Players)
    .set({ turnsBlocked: (player.turnsBlocked || 0) + 1 })
    .where({ ID: player.ID })
})

How to Submit

Reply to the Week 2 blog post on SAP Community with a comment containing:

  1. Your srv/game-service.js handler implementations (paste as a JS code block)
  2. A screenshot showing the HTTP 409 response when calling rollDice in the wrong state
  3. A screenshot or OData response showing your player's position after landing on a snake or ladder
  4. One sentence: what happens if you call winGame on a player whose position is not 100? How does your implementation handle it?

What's Next

In Week 3 — Multiplayer & Events, you add a second player, implement turn rotation, and wire up a CAP event subscriber that automatically advances the game state when a move is confirmed.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
schema.cds

namespace snakeladder;

// ── Status enum — the heart of the state machine ──────────────────────────
type TurnStatus : String enum {
  Waiting;    // Session created, not yet started
  Playing;    // It's this player's turn — roll the dice!
  Moving;     // Dice rolled, player token is being moved
  Blocked;    // Player hit a double-headed snake — skips next turn
  Finished;   // This player has reached square 100 and won
}

// ── Board square — pre-loaded with snake/ladder data ──────────────────────
entity BoardSquares {
  key square      : Integer;        // 1–100
      snakeTo     : Integer;        // 0 if no snake on this square
      ladderTo    : Integer;        // 0 if no ladder on this square
      isDoubleHead: Boolean default false;  // true if this is a double-headed snake mouth
}

// ── Game session ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
entity GameSessions {
  key ID          : UUID;
      name        : String(100);    // e.g. "Friday Afternoon Game"
      createdAt   : Timestamp;
      startedAt   : Timestamp;
      finishedAt  : Timestamp;
      winner      : Association to Players;
}

// ── Player — one row per player per session ───────────────────────────────
@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
  key ID          : UUID;
      session     : Association to GameSessions;
      name        : String(100);
      @readonly
      position    : Integer default 1;      // current square (1–100)
      @readonly
      TurnStatus  : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
      turnOrder   : Integer;                // 1 = goes first
      lastRoll    : Integer;               // last dice result
}

game-service.cds

using { snakeladder } from '../db/schema';

service GameService {

  entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

  @flow.status: TurnStatus
  entity Players as projection on snakeladder.Players actions {

    @from: [ #Waiting ]
    @TO: #Playing
    action startTurn();

    @from: [ #Playing ]
    @TO: #Moving
    action rollDice() returns Integer;

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Waiting                  
    action confirmMove();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Blocked                  // double-headed snake bite
    action blockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Blocked ]
    @TO: $flow.previous            // restores pre-block status
    action unblockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Finished
    action winGame();
  }

  entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

 

 

Kept TurnStatus as it is.

entry.title: Re: Can't access Hands-on Practice for Implementing SAP S...
entry.content:
Hi Claudine,

Short answer: This is not a fault in the practice system — it is an SAP account/login (Universal ID) routing problem. Your sign-in is being redirected to SAP's internal Microsoft Entra tenant ("SAP SE"), where an external learner account cannot authenticate. It normally needs an SAP-side correction, so the fix is to sign in with the correct email-based account and, if that fails, raise a Practice Systems support case.

What the error actually means

It is a Microsoft Entra ID error, not an S/4HANA Cloud error. accounts.sap.com/msec is an application registered in SAP's own corporate Entra tenant ("SAP SE"), and your login is being routed there — but your learner account is not a member of that internal tenant, hence "does not exist in tenant / add as external user first." SAP documents this exact symptom for Universal ID logins that get wrongly redirected to Azure AD. See, for example: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/2121093/how-to-resolve-selected-user-account-does-not-exis

The current login model (context)

Since 20 January 2026, SAP permanently deactivated the old "Sign in with Universal ID" SSO. You must now log in on the SAP sign-in screen with the email address (or S-/P-user ID) plus password connected to your account; the UID is only a wallet now. Likely (inference): re-enrolling on Monday created a fresh SSO binding that is now following the wrong path / resolving your email to an SAP-internal identity — which lands you on the "SAP SE" tenant error. Reference: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3696636

What to try first

You have already cleared your cache and used incognito, so focus on the account, not the browser. On the SAP sign-in screen, choose "Use another account" and type the exact email registered to your SAP Learning account. Do not let the browser or OS auto-select a corporate "Sign in with Microsoft" work account — that auto-selection is what triggers the redirect into the SAP SE tenant.

The reliable fix (support case)

Because the same error hits your colleagues, this is almost certainly a server-side account/routing issue SAP must correct centrally — it is not fully self-fixable. Open a case from the SAP Learning help center with Service Category = "Practice Systems", and include the affected email addresses, user IDs, the course name, and a screenshot of the error. Link: https://learning.sap.com/helpcenter

Summary: The practice system is fine — your login is being misrouted to SAP's internal "SAP SE" Entra tenant. Sign in with the exact email-based SAP account (never a corporate "Sign in with Microsoft" account); and since colleagues are affected too, raise a Practice Systems support case with emails, user IDs, course name and the error screenshot so SAP can correct the routing.

Hope it helps,

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Environmental Details tab missing in Manage Locations — how is it enabled?
entry.content:
I'm trying to assign a Location Classifier to an EHS Location in Environment Management. Per the standard flow, the classifier is maintained in Manage Locations (F3404) → Environmental Details tab → Location Classifiers.

My problem: the Environmental Details tab does not appear on the location. I only see Basic Information / Persons Responsible / Address / Documents. So there's nowhere to add the classifier.

Environment: SAP S/4HANA 2025 (Fully-Activated Appliance / demo tenant).

What I've already checked:

  • The location is Active.
  • A Person Responsible with the Environmental Manager role is assigned on the location.
  • In Customizing, Specify Activities for Persons Responsible in Environment Management, the Manage activity is ticked for the Environmental Manager (person-responsible) role.
  • My user is assigned a copy of SAP_EHSM_HSS_ENVMGR — the role the IMG documentation for "Toggle Visibility of Environmental Details in Locations" names as the one the tab is delivered for. (I also have a copy of SAP_BR_ENVIRONMENTAL_MANAGER, which is what gives me the Manage Locations app.)
  • SU53 shows no authorization failure after the tab fails to appear.

Where I'm stuck: The "Toggle Visibility…" documentation says to open "Specify Roles for Environment Management", add the user role, and select its Manage checkbox. But I can't find any such user-role activity — the only related Customizing activity (Specify Activities for Persons Responsible…) only holds person-responsible role types, not SU01/business roles. So there seems to be no place to register my (copied) user role.

Questions:

  1. What actually controls the visibility of the Environmental Details tab in Manage Locations at runtime?
  2. Since standard SAP roles can't be assigned directly and we work with copies, how is a copy of SAP_EHSM_HSS_ENVMGR meant to be enabled for this tab? Is the tab tied to a specific business catalog / authorization that a role copy might drop (which would explain SU53 being clean)?
  3. Is the "Specify Roles for Environment Management" activity referenced in the documentation actually delivered, or has it been replaced by Specify Activities for Persons Responsible?

Any pointer to the exact authorization object, business catalog, or config that governs this tab would be much appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Project HomeControl 2 · Part 2 — A UI5 frontend, built from non-UI5 Web Components
entry.content:
Impressive, very cool project!

Just a small note: You can actually consume arbitrary web components in UI5 without the need of a custom wrapper UI5 control. If your web component includes a custom-elements.json, the ui5-tooling-modules package can read it and do the wrapping for you. Check out this blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/frontend-ui5-sap-fiori-blog-posts/consuming-your-own-or-external-web-components-in-ui5-applications/ba-p/14281839

Hope to see you at UI5con and chat about this. I will be there and giving a talk about this topic 🙂

entry.title: How to Link Two Analytical Queries in Embedded SAC App? "A linked dimension" Error
entry.content:
How to Link Two Analytical Queries in Embedded SAC App?
"A linked dimension for this model is not included in the visualization's dimension bindings" Error

I am creating an Embedded SAC Application in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and trying to link two Analytical Queries using Company Code. Both Company Code fields have the same data type, semantics, and values, and the link is created successfully.

However, the widget shows the error:

"A linked dimension for this model is not included in the visualization's dimension bindings."

How can I correctly connect/link these two queries? Is there any additional configuration required for linked dimensions or dimension bindings?
