entry.title: Practical SAP LeanIX Modeling Guide
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

Its been a while since I published my last blog in SAP forum. That got quite some attention from the community and helped a lot of them.

Since my last blog post, I have taken up multiple roles, and I am now currently in the Enterprise Architect (EA) role. One of the primary responsibilities of an EA is to document and create artifacts in LeanIX so that it provides Leadership with a holistic picture of their Enterprise.

But sometimes, it gets difficult for Architects to build a good story/dashboard for the customers/clients in LeanIX when there is limited information available. There can be multiple reasons for that – client is too busy, no proper documentation available, LeanIX is considered just another modeling tool, value of LeanIX is not fully understood, too many uncertainty in the attributes data, etc.

I faced this challenge recently. So, it got me thinking on how to tackle this problem.  It was a tricky one because I must build everything from little/nothing while ensuring that the information presented is meaningful and the customer can relate to it.

The first step was to go back to the basics. As Elon Musk says, I go for ‘The First Principles’ method.

First principles thinking is a powerful problem-solving method that strips away assumptions and analogies to reveal fundamental truths. By breaking complex ideas into their most basic, undeniable components, you can reconstruct them in innovative ways rather than just copying what already exists.

The core source of Truth for LeanIX is its LeanIX Documentation. I went in there and put this MetaModel picture in front of me:

I mentally divided this MetaModel into two categories to help me divide-and-conquer this large landscape:

Technical Areas

Business Areas

Application

Objective

IT Component

Initiative

Tech Category

Organization

Provider

Business Capability

Data Object

Business Context

Interface

Platform

The sequence I followed is not arbitrary. Each fact sheet unlocks the next. You cannot meaningfully create a Business Capability without knowing which Applications exist, because the Applications tell you what capabilities the business has. You cannot create a Business Context without Business Capabilities, because Contexts are the end-to-end processes that use those capabilities. You cannot create an Interface without Applications and Data Objects, because an Interface is simply a named data flow between two Applications. The sequence is a dependency chain. Build the leaves before the branches, and the branches before the trunk.

 

Step

Fact Sheet

Depends On

1

Objective

Nothing — start here from public documents

2

System

Contract / subscription list

3

Application

System 

4

IT Component

Application, System

5

Tech Category

IT Component

6

Provider

IT Component, Tech Category 

7

Platform

IT Component, Provider

8

Organization

Public website / org chart research

9

Business Capability

Application, Organization, Reference Catalog

10

Business Context

Business Capability, Application, Organization

11

Initiative

Objective, Application, BC, ITC

12

Data Object

Application, Initiative 

13

Interface

Application, Data Object, IT Component 

14

Contract

Application (link back) 

Rule of thumb: If you find yourself unable to populate the relations for a fact sheet, you have probably created it too early in the sequence. Go back and build its dependencies first.

Objective Factsheet:

Once I had this MetaModel diagram in place, I knew my job was to build a Business Strategy Map for the client. But I needed some documentation from them, like, Mission, Vision, and Values statements, a SWOT Analysis, a Strategic Plan, a Balanced Scorecard (Objectives and KPIs), and Departmental Budgets. I had none of it. So, I started digging into their official website. That’s where a company publishes all its documents. After skimming through the website and various reports, I found most of the needed information in their Annual Report and Quarterly Report. I pulled out the required information from those Reports and created Objective factsheets, populated required attributes, created tags for it and went in to Reports section to ensure its in place.

 

Note: You might have to go back and forth multiple times between your factsheets and Report, and in certain cases, make some assumptions to ensure everything is in place.
I have only shown here a part of the Report as I had to skip the sensitive information of the client. If you are interested in learning how to create the Business Strategy map in a different way, you can read this Blog.

I was quite happy with the Report I generated and verified the various Objective factsheets I created. I then sat and started thinking about what I should do next? I still had no information from the client. One day an idea struck me. I had the contract from the customer, the subscriptions they had and from there I knew what systems they have in their landscape. So, I extracted the Systems details from the contract and subscriptions.

 System Factsheet:

Once I had the all the documents extracted, I filtered out the System details. I initially created one System factsheet, exported it as an excel and then started filling in the details in that excel sheet.

It’s good if you can fill in as many details as possible. I populated the following fields in Systems factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description
  • Sub-Type
  • SAP Details (if an SAP system)

 Once I had the excel sheet updated, I uploaded the excel sheet into the LeanIX tenant and everything was in place. I was quite happy with it. Since I have now created the System factsheet, I know the Application that was present in that System and the IT Component it uses. I can easily identify the Application from the extract I took earlier. And took help with the suggestive AI-capabilities of LeanIX to identify the standard IT Components.

 Application Factsheet:

I started creating the Application factsheet after the System factsheet was in place. I populated the following fields in Application factsheets and linked them to System factsheets.

  • Name
  • Description
  • Sub-Type
  • Subscription
  • Lifecycle Dates
  • 6R Classification
  • Business Criticality
  • Functional Fit
  • Technical Fit
  • Hosting Type
  • Support Type
  • Total Annual Cost

 IT Component Factsheet: 

I now have Application and System factsheets in place. So, I safely went ahead and created IT Component factsheets and linked them to Application and System factsheets.  

I populated the following fields in IT Component factsheets:

  • Name
  • Subtype
  • Description
  • Lifecycle
  • Technology Assessment
  • Tags: Baseline/ Target

I used a two-value tag group — Baseline and Target — on all IT Component fact sheets. This is one of the most powerful low-effort modeling decisions you can make early in the project.

  • Baseline tag = the current, legacy IT Components that the organisation is running today. In my case, these were the on-premise SAP components (SAP NetWeaver, SAP HANA platform, SAP BusinessObjects, BW/4HANA, etc.) that are scheduled to be decommissioned as part of the cloud   transformation.
  • Target tag = the incoming cloud components that will replace them (SAP BTP services, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors SaaS hosting, Integration Suite, etc.).

Once these tags are applied, the IT Component Roadmap report in LeanIX will automatically show you the transition — Baseline components fading out on the left, Target components ramping up on the right — without any additional configuration. This single tag distinction drives the "before and after" story for the entire transformation roadmap.

I moved on to Tech Category and Provider Factsheets now that I have IT Components in place.

 Tech Category Factsheet:

I updated the following fields in Tech Category factsheet and linked it to IT Component factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description

 Provider Factsheet:

I have the IT Component and Tech category in place, so it gets easier to create Provider Factsheet and link it to the IT Component factsheet. I added the following fields in Provider factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description

I then moved on to the Business Area of the landscape and started creating a hierarchical structure of the organization starting with the Organization Name at the top of the hierarchy.

Platform Factsheet:

Now that I had IT Components, Tech Categories, and Providers in place, it was the right time to create Platform fact sheets. Platforms represent the hosting topology — where IT Components physically or virtually run. In my case, I created four Platforms:

  • On-Premise — for legacy SAP NetWeaver, S/4HANA on-premise, and BO components
  • SAP BTP — for all SAP cloud-native and integration components
  • SAP SaaS — for SAP-hosted SaaS applications (SuccessFactors, Ariba, Concur, etc.) M
  • Microsoft SaaS — for Microsoft-hosted components (Fabric, Power BI)

 The key modeling decision: Platform links to IT Components, not to Applications directly. An Application inherits its hosting context through its IT Components. So create Platforms only after IT Components are in place, and link them at the ITC level. I created couple of Platform factsheets and updated the following attributes in them:

  • Name
  • Description

Once I had these fields filled in, I linked the factsheet to various IT Component factsheets.

Organization Factsheet:

I still did not have any information from my client about the Organization. So, I started investigating about a typical Org structure in that industry in which my client operated. Some of the information I could infer from the client’s official website. From there, bit-by-bit I started building the Organizational factsheets and populated the following fields:

  • Name
  • Subtype
  • Description
  • Location
  • Parent (if any)

I then linked the Organization Factsheet to various Application factsheets.  

Business Capability Factsheet:

Since I had the Application factsheet in place along with Organization factsheet, it was a good time to start taking a shot at the Business Capabilities. I knew there was a Reference Catalog offered by LeanIX. I also knew the industry in which my client operated. So, based features an Application provided, I started pulling in L1/L2 Business Capabilities from Reference catalog of LeanIX:

  • Name
  • Description
  • Current Maturity
  • Target Maturity
  • Parent (if any)

Once I had all fields in place, I linked it to all Application factsheets.

 Business Context Factsheet:

Now that I already had Business Capabilities in place, I started taking educated guesses for end-to-end Business Context at a high level. This was the most challenging part for me in all the factsheets. It was not easy to identify various processes that a Business Capability and Application supported. It took me some time to identify some of the processes that were supported and list them out in Business Context factsheet. I then populated the following fields in the factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description

Once I had these in place, I linked it to various Business Capability and Application factsheets.

Here’s a Practical example on how I approached identifying a Business Context:

Step 1 — Start with industry-standard process patterns

Every industry has well-known end-to-end process names. For example, a mining company, the standard processes are immediately recognisable:

Business Context

What it covers

Record to Report

Journal entry → financial close → external reporting          

Source to Pay

Sourcing → purchase order → supplier payment

Recruit to Retire

Talent acquisition → HR → payroll → offboarding

Plan to Produce

Production planning → ore extraction → copper output reporting

Acquire to Decommission

Asset purchase → maintenance → decommission

These are SAP-aligned process patterns (SAP itself uses the same names in its Solution Map). If your client runs SAP, these process names will resonate immediately with both the client's business users and IT team. For non-SAP clients, equivalents exist in APQC, TOGAF, or industry reference architectures.

Tip: Search your client's Annual Report or industry analyst reports for process language. Companies almost always describe their operations using these exact terms in investor presentations — e.g., "procure-to-pay cycle," "hire-to-retire," "order-to-cash."

  ---

Step 2 — Use your Applications as a reverse-lookup

  Each Application you already modelled tells you which process it belongs to. Work backwards:

Application in LeanIX tenant

Business Context it belongs to

SAP SuccessFactors HCM Core, SF ECP, SF WFA

Recruit to Retire

SAP Ariba Procurement, SAP Ariba Sourcing, SAP Business Network

Source to Pay

SAP S/4HANA, SAP BPC

Record to Report

SAP Concur Travel and Expense

Travel and Expense Management

SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity

Treasury and Cash Management

If an Application is hard to place, read its description or look at the Business Capabilities it's linked to — those BCs will point you to the right process family.

  ---

  Step 3 — Add industry-specific processes on top of the standard ones

  Beyond the universal SAP process patterns, every industry has processes unique to it. For mining, these were:

  • Plan to Produce — mine production scheduling and aluminum output, which doesn't exist in a retail or financial services landscape
  • Freight and Logistics Management — aluminum concentrate shipments from mine site to smelter
  • Project and Capital Portfolio Management — capital project governance for mine expansions
  • ESG and Sustainability Reporting — GHG emissions, Aluminum Mark certification, water usage KPIs

These came directly from reading Client’s Annual Report and identifying what makes their business distinct from a generic enterprise.

The rule of thumb: 70% of your Business Contexts will come from standard process patterns. 30% will be industry-specific and will come from the client's own public documents, Annual Report, or strategy presentations.

  ---

  Step 4 — Identify cross-cutting processes last

 Some Business Contexts don't map neatly to a single Business Capability — they cut across multiple ones. In my LeanIX tenant:

  • Enterprise Architecture Management crosses IT Management, Application Development, and Sustainability BCs
  • Business Process Intelligence crosses multiple BCs via the Signavio toolchain

Leave these for last. Once you have all the obvious end-to-end processes mapped, the gaps that remain are usually the cross-cutting ones.

  ---

  Step 5 — Validate by linking, not by thinking

 The real test of whether a Business Context is correctly defined is whether you can link it coherently to:

  • At least one Business Capability (the what)
  • At least one Application (the how)
  • At least one Organisation (the who)

If you can't make all three links for a Business Context, either the context is too vague, or it's actually a sub-process that should be absorbed into a parent context rather than standing alone.

 Initiative Factsheet:

I wanted to give a clear picture of the Enterprise. So, I created various Initiative factsheets based on the Objective factsheets I had from the Business Strategy Map. To achieve those Objectives and based on their size, I divided the Initiatives into short-term, mid-term and long-term categories by putting them under Idea, Program, Project and EPIC sub-categories. I also created milestones in those Initiatives to have realistic dates put to it. This was one of the most time-consuming factsheets I worked on, along with the Business Context factsheet.

I populated the following fields after creating the factsheet:

  • Name
  • Subtype
  • Description
  • Lifecycle
  • Milestones

Once I had these fields populated, I linked it to the Applications, Business Capabilities, IT Components, Objectives, Interfaces and Data Objects.   

Data Object Factsheet:

After having the Initiatives and Application factsheets in place, it is logical to have Data Objects in place. So, based on that I created the Data Objects and populated the following fields:

  • Name
  • Description

I then linked the Application, Interface and Initiative Factsheets to the Data Objects.

Interface Factsheet:

Once I have all the Applications and Data Objects in place, it is time to identify the various interfaces in the landscape. I populated the following fields in the Interface factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description
  • Interface Providers
  • Interface Consumers
  • Data Flow Direction
  • Interface Type
  • Frequency

 Once these were in place, I linked the interface to Data Object, IT Component and Initiative Factsheets.

The most analytically valuable field on an Interface fact sheet is often overlooked: the IT Component link. This field identifies the middleware, the integration platform that physically moves data between the provider and consumer Applications. In my landscape, all interfaces between SAP SuccessFactors systems (HCM Core → ECP, HCM Core → WFA, ECP → WFA) use SAP Integration Suite as the middleware IT Component. Linking it here means that when SAP Integration Suite appears in the ITC Roadmap as retiring or changing, you immediately know which interfaces, and therefore which Application-to-Application data flows are at risk.
Always link the middleware ITC to your interfaces. It turns the Interface Circle Map from a connectivity diagram into a risk and impact analysis tool.

Contract Factsheet:

This is a new factsheet that has been recently introduced by LeanIX Product team. This factsheet is an optional factsheet and used to store information about contracts and the value the contract holds.

The client/customer sometimes wants to see which Business Capability is spending how much money using these Applications. So, I added this factsheet to showcase the cost of various business capabilities.  The Business Capability Cost Report showcases this in form of a horizontal bar chart.

I added the following fields and then linked them to Application factsheets in the ‘Attached Applications’ section of the Contract factsheet:

  • Name
  • Description
  • Contract Financial Information

Dashboards:

Once you have created all the factsheets above and linked them, it is now time to add Reports to Dashboard. Go to the default Dashboard, edit the Dashboard, add the Report panel from the right and select various Reports from the drop-down. After that, every time you log in, you’ll see those selected Reports.

Since I created all these factsheets based on MetaModel and linked them, the following Reports helped me create a clear holistic picture on the Dashboard:

  • Business Strategy Map
  • Business Capability Map
  • High-level Initiative Roadmap
  • Baseline Application Architecture
  • Target Application Architecture
  • Transformation Roadmap
  • Business Capabilities per Application
  • Business objectives mapped to Business Capabilities
  • APM Roadmap
  • Interface Circle Map
  • IT Component Roadmap
  • Lifecycle & Age
  • Business Capability Cost

A note on assumptions and the living model:

  Every EA model built from public information rather than direct client input will contain assumptions. This is not a weakness. It is a feature. A model with structured, documented assumptions is infinitely more useful to a Leadership team than a blank workspace waiting for perfect data.

  The assumptions I made follow a clear hierarchy:

  • Tier 1 (high confidence): Fact sheets derived directly from contracts, subscriptions, or public filings. Application names, IT Component versions, Objective statements from the Annual Report.
  • Tier 2 (medium confidence): Relationships and attributes inferred from industry knowledge. Business Capability linkages, Business Context process names, lifecycle dates aligned to known project timelines.
  • Tier 3 (low confidence, flagged for review): Organizational structure, maturity ratings, and cost allocations where no source document existed.

 The right way to use this model is as a conversation starter, not a final deliverable. Present it to the Leadership team and client Architects with the explicit invitation: "Here is what the data tells us, where does this match your reality, and where does it need to change?" That conversation, guided by a populated model, will produce better outcomes in one meeting than months of waiting for the client to provide perfect data upfront.

 The model is a living artifact. Update it as the conversation evolves.

entry.title: Product Cost Planning at a Reference Site in SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01
entry.content:
Simplifying Product Cost Planning for Retail and Fashion Organizations

Retail and fashion companies typically manage hundreds of stores, multiple distribution centers, and hundreds of thousands—or even millions—of articles. While the procurement and valuation processes across these locations are often identical, Product Cost Planning has traditionally been executed separately for every valuation area.
For organizations operating at this scale, this results in lengthy costing runs, increased database growth, unnecessary system processing, and additional maintenance effort.
To address this challenge, SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 introduces Product Cost Planning at a Reference Site, enabling organizations to calculate product costs once for a designated reference site and subsequently distribute the planned prices to operational sites.
This new capability significantly reduces processing effort while ensuring consistent product valuation across locations that share identical business characteristics.

Why Use a Reference Site for Product Cost Planning?
In many retail organizations, hundreds of DC's/stores purchase the same merchandise under identical procurement conditions.
Consider a typical scenario:
• Every DC/store purchases the same article (with different sizes) from the same vendor.
• Purchase prices are identical. usually at the generic article level.
• Inventory valuation follows the same costing logic.
Despite these similarities, traditional Product Cost Planning executes costing per variant article separately for every valuation area.
For an organization with 500 stores and 120,000 articles, this means generating approximately 60 million costing records, even though the calculated costs are often identical.
The result is longer batch runtimes, higher HANA resource consumption, and unnecessary duplication of data.
With SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01, organizations can now calculate product costs once at a Reference Site and reuse those costs across all eligible receiving sites.
Why Use a Reference Site for Product Cost Planning?
Reference Sites have long been an integral part of SAP Retail. Organizations use them to centralize common master data, reducing maintenance effort and ensuring consistency across operational sites.
Historically, Reference Sites have supported:
• Article master maintenance
• Listing
• Assortments
• Purchasing information
• Pricing
• Logistics master data
With SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01, SAP extends this proven concept to Product Cost Planning.
Rather than creating identical cost estimates for every receiving site, organizations can now:
1. Designate a Reference Site.
2. Perform Product Cost Planning only for that site.
3. Mark the resulting cost estimates.
4. Copy the future planned prices to assigned receiving sites.
5. Release the planned prices centrally.
The enhancement reduces redundant calculations while preserving consistent financial valuation.

How the New Solution Works
Instead of executing Product Cost Planning for every operational site, the calculation is performed once for the Reference Site. The resulting future prices are then distributed to all eligible receiving sites before being released as standard prices.
This reduces duplicated processing while maintaining a single, consistent standard cost across the landscape.

Prerequisites
A prerequisite for using the solution is that the Retail switch (ISR_RETAILSWITCH) is activated.
Using the new material price table, FMLT_PRICE, is optional. The solution functions effectively with or without it.

Step 1 – Assign the Reference Site

Transaction: REFSITE

Create the Reference Site and assign receiving plants.

Reference plant groupings can span multiple company codes, allowing centralized Product Cost Planning across organizational structures.

Step 2 – Edit the Selection List

Transaction: CKMATCON

Generate the material selection list used during Product Cost Planning. You can also implement BAdI BADI_RFM_PROD_COSTING_VARIANT to determine custom logic for identifying the reference material.

Step 3 – Generate the Reference Material Selection List

Transaction: S_ERX_29000720 - Create Selection List for Reference Site

Execute the report to generate a selection list containing only reference materials assigned to the Reference Site.

You can also implement BAdI BADI_RFM_PROD_COSTING_VARIANT to determine custom logic for identifying the reference material.

Step 4 – Execute the Costing Run

Transaction: CK40N

Create the costing run using the newly generated selection list.

Execute the costing process through the Mark step.

At this point, do not release the prices.

Proceed instead to copying future prices.

Step 5 – Copy Future Prices

Transaction: CK_COPY

This report distributes future planned prices from the Reference Site to all receiving plants across all active valuation views.

If company codes use different currencies, the system automatically performs currency conversion.

Two copy options are available:

  • Redistribute All – overwrite all existing planned prices.
  • Incremental Copy – copy only where planned prices do not already exist.
  • Prerequisites
    • Actual Cost Component Split settings must be consistent between reference and receiving plants.
    • Reference materials must be costed and marked for all active valuation views.

Step 6 – Release Future Prices

Transaction: CKME

Reports

  • SAPRCK23_JOB_SPLITTER
  • SAPRCKM_PRCH_JOB_SPLITTER

For large data volumes, SAP recommends using the wrapper reports to split processing into multiple packages, significantly improving release performance.

Example

Suppose an article has:

Cost Component

Value

Purchase Price

$12.00

Freight

$1.00

Handling

$0.50

Standard Cost

$13.50

Instead of calculating this cost estimate separately for every receiving distribution center:

  • DC1001 → $13.50
  • DC1002 → $13.50
  • DC1003 → $13.50
  • ...

SAP performs the calculation once:

Reference Site → $13.50

The planned price is then copied automatically to all eligible receiving sites.

Expected Performance Improvements

The primary objective of this enhancement is not to change the costing logic—it is to eliminate redundant calculations.

Typical improvements observed include:

Metric

Traditional

Reference Site

CK40N Runtime

High

Significantly Reduced

Cost Estimate Volume

Very High

Reduced

HANA Memory Usage

Higher

Lower

The exact improvement depends on:

  • Number of receiving Sites
  • Number of Articles
  • Procurement model
  • System landscape
  • Parallel processing configuration
Considerations Before Adoption
The underlying principle of this solution is simple: if the business conditions are the same, the cost estimate should also be the same.
Consider a retailer selling a T-shirt available in multiple colors but identical in size, procurement source, and purchasing conditions. Whether the T-shirt is gray or white, the calculated standard cost remains unchanged. Likewise, if all sites procure the article under the same commercial and valuation conditions, there is no business justification for generating separate cost estimates for every variant at every store.

Instead, SAP calculates the cost estimate once at the Reference Site and reuses it across all eligible receiving sites, eliminating redundant calculations while ensuring consistent product valuation.

 
Implementation guidelines
  • Group together sites with identical procurement characteristics.
  • Validate purchasing conditions before every costing cycle.
  • Schedule CK40N as a background job.
  • Regularly review cost component splits.
  • Monitor costing logs and exceptions.
Document the business rationale for using shared costing to support governance and audit requirements.

-> Handling Large Data Volumes - 2 Billion Limit Value Reached

As a remind that you need apply file TABLE_PLACEMENT.zip before the reorg as mentioned in this note.

entry.title: Manage Sales Documents App: Introducing Implicit Filters
entry.content:
Background

In this blog, I'm going to write about some implicit filters in the app Manage Sales Documents (MSD).

The Filters

Filter Data via Custom Fields

This is a long-standing impediment in VA05. Now it is tackled partially in MSD.

I assume that there are already custom fields in your systems, and you have been using them in the various sales documents. The expectation is to be able to use them as filters to query data in the list report.

The first thing to do is to Enable the fields for the service of MSD in Custom Fields app.

There are two fields in this example: one for the header, the other for the item.

Custom Field for Sales Document Header

Preparation

Create a field for header:

Enable usage for the service of MSD

Custom Field for Sales Document Item

Preparation

Create a field for item

Enable usage for the service of MSD

Consumption in MSD

Use adapt filters to expose the two filter fields.

The header filter works on all three tabs.

The item filter works on items & schedule lines tabs.

Since it has no effect on Sales Documents tab, as a result, this tab loads all data, an information will be displayed to explain the behavior: Filter "Extended Item Date" is not relevant for tab "Sales Documents". Setting this filter has no effect on the results.'

Sales Document (One-Time Customer) Filter

When using a one-time customer to create sales orders, it is mandatory to change the contact info such as address, name, phone number, email etc. However, the customer number stays the same, thus, it is not possible to precisely filter the documents using just the number. So, we built this filter that can query sales documents via the contact info of a one-time customer.

Open the value help

Filter data via Telephone

Filter data via Name

entry.title: How to show a comparison of price lists or last prices with vendor names from a Purchase Order
entry.content:
Hello,

I have a specific business requirement in SAP Business One. When a user is creating a Purchase Order (PO) and fills in the Item No., they want to see a quick comparison of prices for that item from various suppliers directly from the document row.

Ideally, they want to be able to right-click on the 'Unit Price' column and see an option like "Show Price List" (or similar) which pops up a window showing:

Vendor/Supplier Names

The prices they offer (either from multiple Price Lists or from Last Purchase Prices history).

For now, our standard approach that we already setup uses 'Unit Price' click on the right side mouse column to open "Last Prices", but I prefer to trigger it directly from the 'Unit Price' too but when click on the right side mouse it will show 'Price List' option.

Is there a standard way to achieve this? If not, what is the best customization approach?

Thank you in advance for your insights!

Best Regards,
Ester

entry.title: Re: Project HomeControl 2 · Part 2 — A UI5 frontend, built from non-UI5 Web Components
entry.content:
Thanks Nico — this is exactly the pointer the post needed, and your write-up is the reference I should have linked.

You're right: sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent already does generically what I built by hand — the tag rendering, the property-vs-attribute mutator, forwarding object properties onto the element without a full re-render, slots and events. I went through the source after your comment and it lines up one-to-one with the "pitfalls" I described. So for anyone with a fixed set of components, extending the base class per element is the idiomatic route, and it's a lot less code than my wrapper.

My choice to roll it by hand was deliberate, for two reasons — and I'll make that clearer in the post. First, teaching: writing the glue myself was the only way to show why .data has to be a property and not an attribute, and where the upgrade-order and re-render traps sit. It's easier to appreciate what the base class hands you once you've done it the hard way. Second, the shape: I wanted one generic control that wraps any tag and takes a whole object as a single data property, rather than a typed control per element — handy for a bespoke, fast-moving design system where I'm not maintaining metadata per component. Different trade-off, not a better one.

I'll add a callout near "the bridge" that names the base class and links your article, and frames the hand-rolled version as the under-the-hood explanation rather than the recommended path. Really appreciate you taking the time — this is the kind of comment that makes the Community worth writing for.

entry.title: KKF1报错结算规则无法被创建
entry.content: CO 生产订单KKF1创建时提示结算规则无法创建。我们之前有好几个CO订单类型，我只是新增了订单类型用于新工厂业务。查看了KOT2已经分配了结算规则 具体报错信息如下： 结算规则没能被创建 消息编号 KO311 诊断 只有在把 结算规则 分配到 CO 生产订单（无数量结构的生产订单）后才可以结算该订单发生的实际成本。 在创建期间，系统自动把结算规则分配到该订单。 在此情况下，无法为该订单生成任何结算规则，因为控制该结算规则创建的缺省值尚未存储在系统中。 在您创建该结算规则时系统发出了此错误消息： [KD021]缺省规则  不存在

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud Seamless Planning入門 - Part2 : Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう
entry.content:
このブログシリーズは、SAP Business Data CloudのSeamless Planningの全体像を一通り把握していただくことを目的としています。

このシリーズは、以下で構成されます。
- Part1 : Seamless Planningとは
- Part2 : Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう（本ブログ）
- Part3 : Seamless PlanningでSAP Datasphereの実績ビューをライブバージョンで参照しよう
- to be continued ...

本ブログ「Part2 : Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成してみよう」では、実際にSeamless Planningモデルを作成する手順と、作成後にSAP Datasphere側でデータを確認する方法を解説します。
 

目次

1. Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成
2. ファクトテーブルをSAP Datasphereに公開
3. SAP Datasphereでデータを確認

1. Seamless Planningで計画モデルを作成

SAP Analytics Cloudの計画モデルはデータ保存先を「SAP Analytics Cloud」もしくは「SAP Datasphere」に選択することができます。SAP Datasphereを選択することでSeamless Planningの機能をご利用いただくことができます。

手順：

1.1. SAP Analytics Cloudのナビゲーションバーから 「モデラ」＞新規作成「モデル」＞ 「次へ」を選択します。（今回は「空のモデルから開始」の場合をご案内します）

 1.2. データ保管場所として「SAP Datasphere」を選択し、使用したいスペースを選択して「作成」をクリックします。

1.3.  以降はメジャーやディメンションの設定を行い、モデルを保存します。

これでまずはSAP Datasphereにデータが保管されるSAP Analytics Cloudの計画モデルが作成できました。

 

2. ファクトテーブルをSAP Datasphereに公開

 

SAP Analytics Cloud上で計画モデルを作成・保存しただけでは、SAP DatasphereのData Builder上からファクトテーブルを参照することができません。SAP Analytics Cloud側から「公開」操作を行う必要があります。この操作によって、SAP DatasphereのモデラがSAP Analytics Cloudの計画データをビューや分析モデルのソースとして利用できるようになります。

手順：

2.1. SAP Analytics Cloudで1で作成した計画モデルを開き、右側のモデル詳細パネルにて「SAP Datasphereでの公開」＞「編集...」をクリックします。

 
2.2. 「SAP Datasphereでデータファンデーショnをファクトテーブルとして...1」にチェックが入ってることを確認し、ファクトテーブルの名前を入力して「OK」をクリックします 。

2.3. モデルを保存します。

 これでSAP Analytics Cloudの計画データをSAP Datasphereでも使用できるようになりました。

3. SAP Datasphereでデータを確認

SAP Datasphere側にファクトテーブルが格納されているかを確認してみましょう。

手順：

3.1. SAP Analytics Cloudの右上「製品切り替え」よりSAP Datasphereに切り替えます。

 3.2. ナビゲーションバーの「データビルダ」より対象のスペースを選択すると、2で公開したファクトテーブルを確認することができました。

SAP Datasphereにて、SAP Analytics Cloudの計画データをビューや分析モデルのソースとして利用できるようになります。 

 

以上、Seamless Planningモデルの作成とSAP Datasphereでのデータ確認方法を解説しました。次回のブログでは、SAP Datasphereのビューをライブバージョンとして計画モデルに接続し、実績データをリアルタイム参照する方法を解説します。

参考資料・ブログ

- Expose Objects for Consumption in SAP Datasphere | SAP Help Portal
- Seamless Planning with Datasphere & SAC: Seamless Planning in the Cloud

entry.title: Re: Request for information for SAP Intgeration Suite
entry.content:
Hi @c_walid 

What is the maximum number of concurrent iFlow executions (worker threads): This is depends on memory not iFlow. If I remember correct. Depends on subscription the nodes will be activated. Default it is 4 node can run in parallel from server side. 

When multiple instances of the same iFlow run in parallel, are they queued, rejected, or executed with degraded performance? What is the exact queuing/throttling behavior?: This will be queued based on the memory usage. 

Does concurrent execution of the same iFlow share any runtime resources (JVM heap, thread pool, Camel routes) that could cause performance degradation as concurrency increases?: Yes, it cause the performance issue. 

What is the maximum synchronous HTTP request processing timeout enforced by the Integration Suite runtime (Cloud Foundry router + CPI runtime) for a single message exchange? Is this configurable at the iFlow, tenant, or platform level?: Upto 9999999 milli seconds. Double check it please.

Is there a difference in timeout behavior between the HTTPS Sender adapter and other trigger types (e.g., timer-based, JMS-based)?: HTTPS is for rest ful connection. Timebased is for schedule the iFlow and run as a job. JMS is to Queue the message and re-process. 

If a message exceeds the timeout mid-processing, is there a way to receive a partial-comp: If you use multi-caste in the iFlow, yes it is possible. 

Regards,

Karthik Arjun

entry.title: EXT4 File System Change to XFS File System in SAP HANA Database.
entry.content:
After reading this post,  please check your HANA default File System type. If it is EXT4, please follow this post to change it to XFS.

because HANA does not supported EXT4 File System.

My Operating System is Linux Enterprise Server.

df -hT  command to check File System Type in Linux.

Overview

XFS is designed to give fast performance and handle big data smoothly and It is a high-performance file system used mostly in Linux.  XFS File System is easy to understand  ,  High performance  and  Reads , writes data very fast.

in SAP/HANA environments, XFS is often used for

/hana/data
/hana/log
/hana/shared

 

Below supported filesystems.

GPFS
MPFS*
NFS
OCFS2*
XFS
ext3*
NFS4

Where XFS  is used?

1  Linux servers
2  SAP systems (like HANA environments)
3  Databases
4  Cloud storage
5  Backup systems

 

EXT4 vs XFS Comparison

 

FeatureEXT4XFS
TypeGeneral-purpose file systemHigh-performance file system
Best ForSmall to medium filesLarge files and big data
PerformanceGood for normal workloadsExcellent for heavy workloads
ScalabilityLimited compared to XFSVery high (handles TBs easily)
File SizeSupports large filesSupports very large files efficiently
SpeedGoodFaster for large data operations
JournalingYesYes
RecoveryEasier recovery tools availableRecovery is more complex
UsageOS, apps, general storageDatabases, SAP, large storage systems
Default UseCommon default in LinuxUsed in enterprise environments

 

Team Requirement.

1. SAP Basis Team
Main owner of the activity

Plan downtime and activity steps
Stop & start SAP Application and HANA system
Validate system after change
Coordinate with all teams


2. OS / Linux Team
Responsible for file system change

Create new filesystem (XFS)
Format disks using mkfs.xfs
Mount new file system
Update /etc/fstab
Set correct permissions


3. Backup Team
Ensures data safety

Take full HANA backup before change
Provide restore support if needed

 

Prerequisites.


1. Full System Backup (Mandatory)

Take complete HANA backup (data + log)
Also take OS-level backup if possible.


2. Downtime Planning

File system change requires system downtime
Inform business/users and schedule maintenance window.


3. Check Current File System Type.
df -hT


4. Stop SAP Application and HANA DB Server.
HDB stop
Ensure all HANA services are stopped.

 

Step 1

I am changing here 3 File System Type.

/hana/shared

/hana/data

/hana/log

We can see here ext4 file system showing.

Step 2

Run this command ------zypper install xfsprogs

What is xfsprogs?

xfsprogs  to create, manage, check, and repair XFS file systems on Linux.

xfsprogs has been installed.

Step 3

Take the backup of that File System  which is you want to change.  So i have taken backup of Dir , Files , and all other things which is contain in below File Systems.

/hana/shared

/hana/log

/hana/backup

Step 4

Now Stop the ABAP Application.

Step 5

Now stop Tenant DB from Studio.

ALTER SYSTEM STOP DATABASE SID

 

Step 6 

Now Stop HANA DB

 

Step 7

Check the Process after stop HANA DB

HDB info

HANA DB Stopped

Step 8

Run this command  umount /dev/xvdd    

Target is busy so run this command------à fuser -km /hana/shared

Again, run this command------à      Unmount /dev/xvdd    

Step 9

Now run lsbk command for checking the /hana/shared File System unmount or not.

lsbk

 

We can see in above screenshot xvdd /hana/shared its unmount here.

 

Step 10

Now run this command---  sudo mkfs.xfs -f /dev/xvdd

(for formatting existing partition having different filesystem) its completed.

Step 11

Now change the File System Type. Go to /etc/fstab

Cat /etc/fstab you can see here /hana/shared is ext4

Now open in vi /etc/fstab and change from ext4 to xfs and save :wq!

I red marked under line which is contain ext4 File system now as default.

 

Step 12

Now run this command-----à mount -av its done.

 

Step 13

 

 

Step 14

 

Step 15

Now change fstab file from ext4 to xfs

  Vi /etc/fstab

 

Step 16

mount -av   for checking the mount point

 

Step 17

Copy again all Data in original location and create symbol link.

Cd /usr/sap/HDP

ln -s /hana/shared/HDP/HDB03 /usr/sap/HDP

 

Step 18

After creation link Start the HANA DB.

HDB start

 

 

Step 19

Now start  Tenant DB from Studio.  Tenant DB automatically started when HANA DB started if not started we have to start to manually. 

ALTER SYSTEM START DATABASE H4P

 

Step 20

Now start  Application Server.

Run this command df -hT  to see File system type.

We have xfs File System now.

 

 

 

Conclusion.

Changing from EXT4 to XFS in HANA is recommended to achieve better performance and handle large data efficiently.

Use XFS for HANA systems because it gives better speed and handles large data more efficiently than EXT4.

for Better understanding check this SAP Note 2972496 - SAP HANA Filesystem Types

Please keep this in mind when you are planning to install HANA DB. First check File system Type.

Thanks

entry.title: Re: Can SAP Joule Skills Be Triggered by External Events (e.g., Incoming Email)?
entry.content:
Thanks for your response. I agree that the external event itself is just the trigger.

My intention isn't to use Joule merely as an event-processing engine. The idea is that an external event (such as an email arriving in a shared mailbox) initiates a workflow, and from that point the Joule Agent performs agentic AI tasks—such as understanding the email context, reasoning over enterprise data, deciding the next course of action, orchestrating business processes, interacting with SAP and non-SAP systems, and taking appropriate actions autonomously where applicable.

So my question is specifically about whether Joule Agents/Skills can be invoked programmatically by an external event, instead of only through a user interaction in the Joule interface. I'm interested in understanding if SAP currently supports this event-driven invocation pattern or if there are recommended architectures to achieve it.

entry.title: Default User Layout in Material Documents Overview App
entry.content:
Dear Team,

We would like to set a user-specific default layout in the Material Documents Overview app so that the same layout is automatically applied whenever the app is accessed, either directly or indirectly.

We tested this behavior in the SAP Public Cloud environment. The default layout works correctly when the app is accessed directly. However, when the Material Documents Overview app is launched from another application, such as Display Stock Overview (MMBE), the system resets the layout to the standard default instead of retaining the user-specific layout.

As a result, users must manually select and apply their preferred layout each time, which impacts reporting consistency and requires additional time and effort.

Please confirm whether this is the standard system behavior or if there is a configuration or enhancement available to ensure that the user-specific default layout is retained regardless of how the app is accessed?

entry.title: Re: Can SAP Joule Skills Be Triggered by External Events (e.g., Incoming Email)?
entry.content:
Thanks for the suggestion. I agree that SAP Business Process Automation is a good fit for handling the event trigger and workflow orchestration.

My question is more about what happens after that. For example, BPA detects the incoming email and starts a process, but instead of implementing all the business logic in the workflow, can it invoke a Joule Agent to perform agentic AI tasks such as analyzing the email, reasoning over enterprise context, deciding the next best action, and then returning the outcome to the process?

I'm trying to understand whether this pattern—BPA for event orchestration + Joule Agent for AI-driven reasoning and execution—is supported today or if SAP recommends a different architecture.

entry.title: Re: Writing (Posting) Data to Non-Leaf Members in Advance...
entry.content:
@79 

By default Its not possible to write as a target to non leaf members of a dimension. Since parents are supposed to show the aggregation of members below it.

To be able to write to a non leaf member, you would need to create a alternate hierarchy where these non leaf nodes are leaf. In your Data action you need to point to this alternate hierarchy and it will allow you to post values on that.

The concept is explained in the below section of help document.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/00f68c2e08b941f081002fd3691d86a7/1679129715024574b7bbb4a611471765.html?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=release

 

Regarding your design, you could first aggregate the data by posting on parent members using alternate hierarchy. Maintain attributes for cost breakdown derivation on these parents and then finally derive the cost breakdown on this aggregated data. Once derivation is complete and reconciled, you could delete the intermediate data at parents without cost breakdown ( ="#")

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: Query on Creating a Generic OData Connection in SAP A...

@arjun_tko 

Its not possible to work with a generic Odata connection in SAC. Only a single OData service per connection can be consumed and you must provide the connection with a full OData service path per OData connection.

Its covered in the below Note Point 8, Note 2.

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2628688

2628688 - OData service connection in SAP Analytics Cloud ***Collective KBA***

 

Hope this helps!

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: Query on Creating a Generic OData Connection in SAP A...
entry.content:
Hello @arjun_tko 

A single generic SAC OData connection cannot be used for multiple OData services. Separate connections must be created for each OData service URL.

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Writing (Posting) Data to Non-Leaf Members in Advance...
entry.content:
Hello @79 

No, Advanced Formulas cannot write data directly to non-leaf (parent) members. Data can only be written to leaf/base members.

Create a separate mapping dimension/model containing:

G/L Account (or Account Group)
Organization (or Organization Group)
Business
Cost Breakdown Category
Use RESULTLOOKUP() and LINK() in Advanced Formulas to assign the category.


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Forms Label Translation
entry.content:
Hello @Reserve 

There is no standard translation application, CDS view, or API in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition that automatically translates arbitrary form labels (e.g., English → Dutch) based on input text.
Maintain multilingual texts directly in SAP Forms Service by Adobe templates.


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: API-Centric Integration in SAP Integration Suite: A new paradigm for API & Integration practitioner
entry.content:
This blog is co-authored with @artigopalan (PM Director, SAP Integration Suite)

Introduction

Working with SAP Integration Suite, you know the depth it brings across its two most valued capabilities: Cloud Integration (CPI), the engine behind message mediation, transformation, and process orchestration, and API Management, where APIs are governed, secured, protected, and published. Together, they power and integrate some of the most complex enterprise landscapes in the world. And yet, as mature and capable as each is in its own right, there's a natural question that practitioners regularly ask: what if these two capabilities truly converged? What if the design experience, the runtime, and the observability layer spoke a single language, one that felt native to both the integration developer and the API architect? That question is what API-Centric Integration is designed to answer.

One Coin. Two Sides. One Experience.

Practitioners have long known that API management and integration are not independent concerns. Every API is an integration surface. Every integration exposes a contract that could benefit from API-level governance. The separation has been more a consequence of how solutions and their technical stacks evolved than a reflection of how the work actually flows.

API-Centric Integration is built on a simple but powerful premise: the design language, the runtime, and the observability plane should be unified, empowered by AI. Not loosely coupled. Not bridged after the fact. Unified by design.

What This Means in Practice

A common design language

Customers can now define, secure, and manage APIs using a single, consistent authoring experience that natively supports mediation and augmentation steps. The modeling itself occurs on a newly introduced artifact type simply called the API, and this, too, has been consciously kept distinct. It is not the same as an IFlow in Cloud Integration, and it is not the same as the API proxy or API artifact you may know from the classic API Management functionality. It is a first-principles rethink of what an API artifact should look like when integration capability is native to it, not layered on top.

If you are a Cloud Integration practitioner, the concepts inside it will feel familiar: routing, transformation, enrichment, logging. If you are an API architect, the governance constructs like policies, rate limiting, and security schemes are first-class citizens. The two disciplines that have long lived on separate stacks, with separate tools and separate mental models, now meet inside a single artifact.

Underneath this experience runs a single, SAP-managed cloud-native runtime called the Integration Cell. It exists to make the unified design experience above possible, with the deployment, scaling, and execution details handled for you.

 

Agent-readiness, with MCP Gateway

This is perhaps the most forward-looking aspect of API-Centric Integration. APIs built within this new model can be elevated to MCP Servers, the protocol standard for exposing tools and data to AI agents and large language models, without additional development effort. It is a deliberate architectural choice to ensure that the APIs you build today are natively consumable by the agentic applications and Joule-powered workflows of tomorrow. The API you publish for a human consumer and the tools you expose to an AI agent are, again, two sides of the same coin. 

Follow this post to learn more about the MCP Gateway.

 

A single observability pane

One of the most persistent operational challenges in running both stacks has been complexity: separate monitoring dashboards, separate log pipelines, and no single place to understand what is actually happening across your API and integration landscape.

API-Centric Integration changes that. It runs on a single execution engine with a unified pipeline for logs and traces. When something needs attention, you look in one place.

But observability in API-Centric Integration goes further than just consolidating logs. The same unified pane brings in analytics capabilities that were previously either absent or fragmented. Usage patterns, traffic trends, and performance metrics across your APIs and MCP servers are visible together, in a single dashboard, broken down by the dimensions that actually matter: by API, by consumer, by agent, by tool.

For teams that need to stay ahead of problems rather than just react to them, the analytics layer includes AI-based anomaly detection that learns the normal traffic behaviour of your APIs and flags deviations before they become incidents. Alongside that, traffic predictions give you a forward-looking view of expected API call volumes, so capacity planning and resource decisions are grounded in data rather than guesswork.

The result is an observability experience that is not just unified, but genuinely intelligent. One place to look, whether you are investigating a live issue, reviewing consumption trends, or planning for what comes next.

A simplified commercial model

For integrations built with this new design, there is no double-counting across the API and integration layers. You are running one thing. You pay for one thing.

A foundation for AI Gateway capabilities

Beyond MCP, a rich AI Gateway will be integrated into this design experience over time, bringing model routing, token governance, and LLM observability into the same unified surface. The integration layer is becoming the AI governance layer. API-Centric Integration is where that convergence begins.

What this is, and what it isn't

API-Centric Integration is relevant for greenfield projects: new APIs, new integration flows, new architectures built on a clean slate. Existing APIs managed through the current API Management capability and existing CPI iFlows continue to operate exactly as they are. There is no forced migration. No disruption to what is running in production.

Nothing existing is being sunset, deprecated, or disturbed. Think of it as the recommended starting point for net-new work, the architecture you would choose if you were starting today, knowing what you now know about where integration and AI are heading.

One additional note on scope: the Integration Cell is a new runtime introduced specifically for this paradigm. It sits alongside, not on top of, your existing Cloud Integration runtime and Edge Integration Cell. These are not competing runtimes. They are the right tool for the right job, depending on where you are in your journey.

The bigger picture

SAP Integration Suite has always been the connective tissue of enterprise landscapes. What is changing is the nature of what needs to be connected and what needs to consume those connections. Business processes increasingly involve AI agents that require governed, observable, low-latency access to enterprise data and functions. In that world, the distinction between an integration runtime and an API runtime becomes not just unnecessary, but actively counterproductive.

API-Centric Integration is our answer to that shift: a unified foundation that respects the depth of what practitioners have built, while opening the door to a design paradigm that is ready for the agent-first era.

Getting started

API-Centric Integration is available now as part of SAP Integration Suite. Full product documentation, including a guided walkthrough of the API Artifact, policy modelling, and Integration Cell setup, is available on the SAP Help Portal:

API-Centric Integration - SAP Help Portal 

Rollout status

The rollout is actively in progress across SAP's managed landscape:

  • Trial landscape: Already updated and available for exploration today.
  • Stage 1 data centers: Already updated and available for exploration today.
  • Stage 2 data centers: Currently receiving software updates and feature enablement.

For the full rollout schedule and data center details, refer to SAP Note 3418154 .

If you are an existing SAP Integration Suite customer, no action is required on your part to receive the update. When the rollout reaches your data center, the capability will be available in your tenant. Your existing integration flows and API proxies are unaffected.

We will be sharing more on hands-on guidance, and product deep-dives in the coming weeks. Questions and feedback are welcome in the SAP Community comments below.

entry.title: Built an MCP Server for SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

I'm Jayesh Sinha, an SAP Integration Suite Architect with over 11 years of experience designing and implementing integration solutions across SAP and non-SAP landscapes. Throughout my career, I have worked extensively with SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), helping organizations build scalable, reliable, and maintainable integration flows. This hands-on experience with CPI's monitoring, troubleshooting, and operational challenges is what inspired me to explore how AI agents and the Model Context Protocol can simplify day-to-day integration work. This blog reflects my journey of combining enterprise integration expertise with modern AI tooling to make CPI operations more conversational and efficient.

SAP Cloud Integration, also known as SAP CI, is widely used for designing, deploying, and monitoring integration flows between SAP and non-SAP systems. While CPI provides strong monitoring and troubleshooting capabilities, checking message logs, failed messages, runtime artifacts, and integration packages usually requires manual navigation inside the SAP Integration Suite UI.

In this blog, we will build a Python-based MCP server that connects to SAP CI and exposes CI operations as tools. Then, we will connect an AI agent to this MCP server so the agent can inspect CI logs, identify failed messages, summarize issues, and help with troubleshooting.

Instead of manually opening CPI, filtering logs, and reading technical payloads, the AI agent can call predefined CPI tools through the MCP server and return a clear summary.

What Is MCP?

MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a standard way for AI applications and agents to connect with external tools, systems, APIs, files, and data sources.

In simple terms, MCP allows an AI agent to use tools safely and consistently.

For example, instead of giving an AI model direct access to SAP CPI credentials and asking it to figure out API calls, we create an MCP server that exposes controlled tools

High-Level Architecture

User Queation -> AI Agent-> MCP Client -> Python MCP Server -> SAP CPI OData APIs -> CPI Logs, Artifacts, Packages, Metadata

The MCP server is responsible for:

Authenticating with SAP CPI
Calling CPI OData APIs
Handling JSON responses
Exposing CPI actions as MCP tools
Keeping credentials outside the AI prompt
Providing a controlled access layer

Benefits of This Approach

This solution provides several benefits.

It makes CPI troubleshooting more conversational. Developers can ask questions instead of manually navigating logs.

It separates AI reasoning from enterprise system access. The AI agent does not directly handle CPI credentials.

It creates a reusable integration layer. The same MCP server can be used by different MCP-compatible clients.

It improves maintainability. CPI API logic is centralized in one server.

It is extensible. More CPI tools can be added later as needed.

It supports safer automation. Read-only tools can be introduced first, while write operations can be added later with stronger controls.

Configuration

you can use CHATGPT/CLAUDE Desktop to connect to the MCP server.

1) Download MCP server in you local system from below GIT repository.

https://github.com/jacksnh/CPI-MCP-SERVER 

2) Modify "claude_desktop_config.json" to add the MCP server

"ci-mcp-server2": {
    "command": "python",
    "args": [
        "C:\\Users\\Username\\path\\to\\your\\server.py"
    ]
}

3) Restart Claude desktop and you will see below Server in Developer Section

4) Now your AI agent is ready to interact with MCP server

 

Conclusion

Integrating SAP Cloud Integration with an AI agent through an MCP server represents a meaningful step forward in how developers and integration teams interact with enterprise middleware. Rather than manually sifting through logs and navigating the SAP Integration Suite UI, teams can now ask plain-language questions and receive clear, actionable answers in seconds.

entry.title: MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite: Your APIs, Ready for the Age of Agents
entry.content:
This blog is co-authored with @artigopalan  (PM Director, SAP Integration Suite)

This post is a follow-up to our earlier introduction of API-Centric Integration. If you haven't read that yet, we recommend starting there.

Introduction

The way enterprise software gets used is changing. AI agents, autonomous programs that reason, plan, and take action, are no longer a future concept. They are arriving in production environments now, and they need to access your enterprise systems, data, and processes to be useful.

The question for every enterprise architect and IT leader is the same: how do you let AI agents into your systems without losing the governance and control you have spent years building?

In our previous post, we introduced API-Centric Integration: a unified design and runtime experience that brings API governance and integration mediation together in a single artifact. We closed with a forward-looking note on agent-readiness. This post is where that story continues.

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is the answer.

A quick glossary before we begin

MCP is still a relatively new term in most enterprise conversations, so it is worth grounding the three concepts that matter here before going further.

An MCP Tool is a named, callable entity that an AI agent can discover and invoke. Think of it as a function with a description: the agent reads that description, decides whether the tool is relevant to the task at hand, and calls it. A tool might represent "Get Customer Order Status," "Reconcile Inventory Levels Across Distribution Centers," or "Trigger a Purchase Order Approval." Tools are the atomic unit of agent-accessible functionality.

An MCP Server is the runtime host that exposes one or more tools over the Model Context Protocol, an open standard that any MCP-compatible agent or framework can connect to. If tools are the endpoints, the MCP Server is the API server hosting them. It makes tools discoverable and invokable in a standardized way, decoupled from how the underlying system actually works.

An MCP Gateway is the enterprise control plane for MCP. Where an MCP Server hosts tools, the Gateway governs access to them: enforcing authentication, authorization, rate limiting, and payload protection, and providing full monitoring and traceability. SAP Integration Suite's MCP Gateway aggregates tools from SAP APIs, non-SAP APIs, integration flows, data sources, and external MCP Servers into a single governed entry point.

The problem with the way things work today - and why your existing investment is the solution

Most enterprises that have invested in SAP Integration Suite already have a curated, governed library of APIs: connecting SAP S/4HANA, Business AI Platform, third-party systems, and custom backends. That represents years of work: designing integrations, securing them, documenting them, and operationalizing them.

The challenge until now has been that none of that investment was directly accessible to AI agents. Agents do not call REST APIs natively the way developers do. They need tools: described, discoverable, and callable via a standardized protocol. Without that, every agent project forces the same painful workaround: a developer manually reads the backend schema and translates it into the agent's tool-calling code, field names, types, nested structures, all hardcoded. That mapping is brittle. If the backend schema changes, nothing tells the agent. The integration breaks, silently. The next project repeats the same work from zero. And security and governance are only as consistent as each developer's diligence.

The agent can reason. It still cannot reach your systems without someone hand-building the bridge, every time.

MCP Gateway closes that gap without asking you to rebuild anything. Your curated APIs become agent-ready tools. Your existing governance models: RBAC, rate limits, and monitoring carry forward into the agentic layer. The work you have done is not a legacy to migrate away from. It is the platform your AI strategy runs on.

Introducing MCP Gateway

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is organized around three things:

Connect. Turn APIs and services into discoverable, reusable tools for agent use. API specifications are automatically translated into MCP schema and augmented with natural language semantics, so agents understand not just the structure of a tool but what it is for. No manual schema translation. No hardcoding. Backend connectivity follows familiar conventions: API Providers and BTP Destinations are used to discover and connect to SAP and non-SAP backend systems, so the connectivity model your teams already rely on carries forward into the agentic layer. Developer Hub extends naturally from an API catalog to a tool and MCP catalog: developers and agent builders can discover available MCP tools, understand their purpose and schema, and subscribe, using the same experience they already use for APIs

Govern. Apply policies, authentication, and usage controls for agents to access tools and systems. Rate limiting, IP blocking, authentication based on OIDC from SAP  (XSUAA) and third-party identity providers, and payload protection all apply to agent traffic with the same rigor as human-facing API traffic. Agents only see and can invoke the tools they are explicitly authorised for: essential in multi-agent environments where different agents have different roles and should have different access boundaries.

Analyze. Provide visibility and traceability across agents, tools, and enterprise landscapes. Every tool invocation by every agent is logged with full traceability, supporting compliance requirements, operational governance, and usage analytics. You have the same visibility into agent behavior that you have today into the transactions handled by SAP Integration Suite, including comprehensive logs, traces, and intelligent analytics.

 

MCP Gateway as the endorsed pathway for SAP API access

This is not a standalone capability sitting outside SAP's broader architecture thinking. SAP's Architecture Center reference architecture for A2A and MCP for Interoperability defines how enterprise AI agent ecosystems should be structured, and SAP Integration Suite's MCP Gateway sits at its core.

In practical terms, if you are deploying AI agents that interact with SAP systems, MCP Gateway is not just a convenient option. It is SAP's architecturally and policy-endorsed approach to doing so in a compliant, governed, and auditable way.

The recommendation is clear: external third-party agents should use MCP Gateway to access SAP systems. Customer-managed pro-code and low-code agents deployed in the SAP BTP context should use MCP Gateway to access SAP systems. Third-party MCP Servers should federate with the MCP Gateway to access SAP systems.

An important distinction: MCP is a connectivity protocol, not a semantic layer

The Architecture Center makes a point here that is easy to overlook and consequential if you do.

MCP standardizes how agents call tools and APIs. It does not add business context or improve the accuracy of AI results. Connecting an MCP server directly to raw SAP transactional APIs without semantic enrichment leads to poor entity discovery accuracy, excessive token consumption, and, for write operations, a real risk of incorrect business transactions. An agent that calls a raw OData endpoint without understanding what the fields mean, or without the business context to distinguish a purchase order from a blanket agreement, will reason poorly and act incorrectly.

This is why the design of an MCP tool matters as much as the platform that hosts it. MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite handles the governed, enterprise-grade concerns: authentication, authorization, rate limiting, payload protection, and observability across SAP and non-SAP APIs. What it does not do, and is not designed to do -> is to decide how to shape your tools.

An API Artifact that exposes nine atomic CRUD endpoints as nine separate tools will produce an MCP Server that forces an agent to invent the orchestration logic a good tool designer should have encoded. The right instinct is to design tools around business outcomes rather than API operations: find_at_risk_suppliers_for_material rather than GET /assmnt/PlantMaterialRisk/{plantId}. The platform enables either. The design is yours.

Two typical use cases

The following scenarios show what becomes possible when your existing SAP investment meets the agentic layer.

Use Case 1: Third-Party AI Platform Accessing SAP Business Data

A retailer uses Microsoft Copilot Studio to build procurement agents for their buying team. The agents need to query SAP product catalogs, check supplier agreements, and initiate purchase requisitions. Historically, this required a custom integration project where developers hand-translated backend schemas into tool-calling code.

With the MCP Gateway, SAP's procurement APIs are exposed as MCP tools through a hosted MCP Server. Copilot Studio agents connect via the standard MCP protocol, authenticate via OIDC, and invoke only the tools they are authorized for. The IT team governs access from a single control plane in Integration Suite, fully aligned with the endorsed architecture from SAP's Architecture Center.

Use Case 2: Multi-Agent HR Process Automation on SAP BTP

An enterprise deploys multiple specialized agents on SAP BTP for HR: one for onboarding, one for payroll queries, one for performance data. Each is built differently: the onboarding agent is low-code via Joule Studio (fka SAP Build), the payroll agent is pro-code using SAP Cloud SDK for AI.

Both agents access SAP SuccessFactors and third-party HR data sources via the MCP Gateway. Tool authorization is scoped per agent role: the onboarding agent cannot access payroll tools, and vice versa. The architecture follows SAP's golden path for agentic AI interoperability.

What it shields you from

MCP is still evolving rapidly. The specification has already undergone significant architectural changes in 2026. Running self-managed MCP servers means tracking that specification roadmap closely, managing upgrade cycles, and absorbing the security implications of each revision. Both patterns of self-managed MCP servers, whether hosted externally or on SAP BTP, place the full operational responsibility for platform stability, security hardening, credential lifecycle, and spec compatibility squarely on the customer or partner deploying them.

For a detailed breakdown of the cross-cutting concerns every MCP operator must address, including authentication, token exchange, rate limiting, observability, and lifecycle management, the Third-Party MCP Access reference architecture on SAP's Architecture Center is the definitive starting point.

The bigger picture

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is the natural evolution of the API Gateway into the agentic era. The core promise is the same: bring your systems together under governed, observable, secure access. The difference is that the consumer is no longer just a developer or an application. It is an AI agent.

SAP Integration Suite has always been the connective tissue of enterprise landscapes. MCP Gateway is what makes that connective tissue agent-aware. The APIs you have already built, the integration flows already running, the S/4HANA function modules already deployed: all of it can now be made consumable by agents through a single governed surface, with the same authentication, observability, and policy enforcement you apply to human-facing traffic today.

Getting started

MCP Gateway is available now as part of SAP Integration Suite Premium and Enhanced Editions. Full documentation is available on the SAP Help Portal. You can also get started by trying it out using the SAP BTP trial or the Free Service Plan.

We will be sharing deep dives and hands-on tutorials in the coming weeks. Questions and feedback are welcome in the SAP Community comments below.

entry.title: Re: C_AIG SAP Generative AI Developer Exam — Tips for the System-Based Assessment
entry.content: You can use Joule,  so I don't see why not (best to check with the chaps at SAP  learning, though). You really need to do this a couple of times on the practice systems before attempting the exam proper, that's the best advise I can give you.
